subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If a matrix has 28 elements, what are the possible orders it can have? What if it has 13 elements?
question_html: If a matrix has 28 elements, what are the possible orders it can have? What if it has 13 elements?
solution_text: We know that, if a matrix is of order {tex}m \times n{/tex} it has mn elements, where m and n are natural numbers. We have, {tex}m \times n = 28{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (m, n) = {(1, 28), (2, 14), (4, 7), (7, 4), (14, 2), (28,1)} So, the possible orders are {tex}1 \times 28,\;2 \times 14,\;4 \times 7,\;7 \times 4,\;14 \times 2,\;28 \times 1{/tex} Also, if it has 13 elements, then {tex}m \times n = 13{/tex} (m, n) = {(1, 13), (13, 1)} Hence, the possible orders are {tex}1 \times 13,\;13 \times 1{/tex} .
solution_html:
We know that, if a matrix is of order {tex}m \times n{/tex} it has mn elements, where m and n are natural numbers.
We have, {tex}m \times n = 28{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (m, n) = {(1, 28), (2, 14), (4, 7), (7, 4), (14, 2), (28,1)}
So, the possible orders are {tex}1 \times 28,\;2 \times 14,\;4 \times 7,\;7 \times 4,\;14 \times 2,\;28 \times 1{/tex}
Also, if it has 13 elements, then {tex}m \times n = 13{/tex}
(m, n) = {(1, 13), (13, 1)}
Hence, the possible orders are {tex}1 \times 13,\;13 \times 1{/tex}.

question_text: In the matrix {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&1&x \\ 2&{\sqrt 3 }&{{x^2} - y} \\ 0&5&{\frac{{ - 2}}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , write The order of the matrix A The number of elements Write elements a 23 , a 31 , a 12
question_html:
In the matrix {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&1&x \\ 2&{\sqrt 3 }&{{x^2} - y} \\ 0&5&{\frac{{ - 2}}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, write

  1. The order of the matrix A
  2. The number of elements
  3. Write elements a23, a31, a12
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&1&x \\ 2&{\sqrt 3 }&{{x^2} - y} \\ 0&5&{\frac{{ - 2}}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} the order of matrix {tex}A = 3 \times 3{/tex} the number of elements {tex}= 3 \times 3 = 9{/tex} [Since, the number of elements in an {tex}m \times n{/tex} matrix will be equal to {tex}m \times n = mn{/tex} ] Since, aij is the element lying ion the ith and jth column, we have a 23 = x 2 - y, a 31 = 0, a 12 = 1
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&1&x \\ 2&{\sqrt 3 }&{{x^2} - y} \\ 0&5&{\frac{{ - 2}}{5}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

  1. the order of matrix {tex}A = 3 \times 3{/tex}
  2. the number of elements {tex}= 3 \times 3 = 9{/tex} [Since, the number of elements in an {tex}m \times n{/tex} matrix will be equal to {tex}m \times n = mn{/tex}]
  3. Since, aij is the element lying ion the ith and jth column, we have a23 = x2 - y, a31 = 0, a12 = 1

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Construct {tex}{A_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} matrix, where {tex}{a_{ij}} = \frac{{{{(i - 2j)}^2}}}{2}{/tex}
question_html: Construct {tex}{A_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} matrix, where {tex}{a_{ij}} = \frac{{{{(i - 2j)}^2}}}{2}{/tex}
solution_text: We know that, the notation, namely {tex}A=[a_{ij}]_{m×n}{/tex} indicates that A is a matrix of order {tex}m \times n{/tex} , also {tex}1 \leq i \leq m,\;1 \leq j \leq n;\;i,\;j \in N{/tex} . Here, {tex}A = {\left[ {{a_{ij}}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} where, {tex}a_{ij}= \frac{{(i - 2j)}^2}{2},\;1 \leq i \leq 2;\;1 \leq j \leq2{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\therefore {a_{11}} = \frac{{{{(1 - 2)}^2}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{a_{12}} = \frac{{{{(1 - 2 \times 2)}^2}}}{2} = \frac{9}{2}{/tex} {tex}{a_{21}} = \frac{{{{(2 - 2 \times 1)}^2}}}{2} = 0{/tex} {tex}{a_{22}} = \frac{{{{\left( {2 - 2 \times {2}} \right)}^2}}}{2} = 2{/tex} Thus, {tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}}&{\frac{9}{2}} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that, the notation, namely {tex}A=[a_{ij}]_{m×n}{/tex} indicates that A is a matrix of order {tex}m \times n{/tex}, also {tex}1 \leq i \leq m,\;1 \leq j \leq n;\;i,\;j \in N{/tex}.
Here, {tex}A = {\left[ {{a_{ij}}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex}
where, {tex}a_{ij}= \frac{{(i - 2j)}^2}{2},\;1 \leq i \leq 2;\;1 \leq j \leq2{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\therefore {a_{11}} = \frac{{{{(1 - 2)}^2}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{a_{12}} = \frac{{{{(1 - 2 \times 2)}^2}}}{2} = \frac{9}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{a_{21}} = \frac{{{{(2 - 2 \times 1)}^2}}}{2} = 0{/tex}
{tex}{a_{22}} = \frac{{{{\left( {2 - 2 \times {2}} \right)}^2}}}{2} = 2{/tex}
Thus, {tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{1}{2}}&{\frac{9}{2}} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Construct {tex}{A_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} matrix, where {tex}{a_{ij}} = \left| { - 2i + 3j} \right|{/tex}
question_html: Construct {tex}{A_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} matrix, where {tex}{a_{ij}} = \left| { - 2i + 3j} \right|{/tex}
solution_text: We know that, the notation, namely {tex}A=[a_{ij}]_{m×n}{/tex} indicates that A is a matrix of order {tex}m \times n{/tex} , Also {tex}1 \leq i \leq m,\;1 \leq j \leq n;\;i,\;j \in N{/tex} . Here, {tex}A = {\left[ {{a_{ij}}} \right]_{2 \times 2}} = \left| { - 2i + 3j} \right|,\;1 \leq i \leq 2;\;1 \leq j \le 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore {a_{11}} = \left| { - 2 \times 1 + 3 \times 1} \right| = 1{/tex} {tex}{a_{12}} = \left| { - 2 \times 1 + 3 \times 2} \right| = 4 \: \:\left[ {\because \left| { - 1} \right| = 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}{a_{21}} = \left| { - 2 \times 2 + 3 \times 1} \right| = 1{/tex} {tex}{a_{22}} = \left| { - 2 \times 2 + 3 \times 2} \right| = 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}A=\begin{bmatrix}1&4\\1&2\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that, the notation, namely {tex}A=[a_{ij}]_{m×n}{/tex} indicates that A is a matrix of order {tex}m \times n{/tex},
Also {tex}1 \leq i \leq m,\;1 \leq j \leq n;\;i,\;j \in N{/tex}.
Here, {tex}A = {\left[ {{a_{ij}}} \right]_{2 \times 2}} = \left| { - 2i + 3j} \right|,\;1 \leq i \leq 2;\;1 \leq j \le 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {a_{11}} = \left| { - 2 \times 1 + 3 \times 1} \right| = 1{/tex}
{tex}{a_{12}} = \left| { - 2 \times 1 + 3 \times 2} \right| = 4 \: \:\left[ {\because \left| { - 1} \right| = 1} \right]{/tex}
{tex}{a_{21}} = \left| { - 2 \times 2 + 3 \times 1} \right| = 1{/tex}
{tex}{a_{22}} = \left| { - 2 \times 2 + 3 \times 2} \right| = 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}A=\begin{bmatrix}1&4\\1&2\end{bmatrix}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Construct a {tex}3 \times 2{/tex} matrix whose elements are given by {tex}a_{ij}=e^{ix}sin{jx}{/tex}
question_html: Construct a {tex}3 \times 2{/tex} matrix whose elements are given by {tex}a_{ij}=e^{ix}sin{jx}{/tex}
solution_text: Since, {tex}A = {\left[ {{a_{ij}}} \right]_{m \times n}}1 \leqslant i \leqslant m{/tex} and {tex}1 \leqslant j \leqslant n,i,j \in N{/tex} {tex}\therefore A = {\left[ {{e^{ix}}\sin jx} \right]_{3 \times 2}};1 \leqslant i \leqslant 3;\;1 \leqslant j \leqslant 2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {a_{11}} = {e^{1x}}.\sin 1.x = {e^x}\sin x{/tex} {tex}{a_{12}} = {e^{1x}}.\sin 2.x = {e^x}\sin 2x{/tex} {tex}{a_{21}} = {e^{2x}}.\sin 1.x = {e^{2x}}\sin x{/tex} {tex}{a_{22}} = {e^{2x}}.\sin 2.x = {e^{2x}}\sin 2x{/tex} {tex}{a_{31}} = {e^{3x}}.\sin 1.x = {e^{3x}}\sin x{/tex} {tex}{a_{32}} = {e^{3x}}.\sin 2x = {e^{3x}}\sin 2x{/tex} {tex}\therefore A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{e^x}\sin x}&{{e^x}\sin 2x} \\ {{e^{2x}}\sin x}&{{e^{2x}}\sin 2x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{e^{3x}}\sin x}&{{e^{3x}}\sin 2x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, {tex}A = {\left[ {{a_{ij}}} \right]_{m \times n}}1 \leqslant i \leqslant m{/tex} and {tex}1 \leqslant j \leqslant n,i,j \in N{/tex}
{tex}\therefore A = {\left[ {{e^{ix}}\sin jx} \right]_{3 \times 2}};1 \leqslant i \leqslant 3;\;1 \leqslant j \leqslant 2{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {a_{11}} = {e^{1x}}.\sin 1.x = {e^x}\sin x{/tex}
{tex}{a_{12}} = {e^{1x}}.\sin 2.x = {e^x}\sin 2x{/tex}
{tex}{a_{21}} = {e^{2x}}.\sin 1.x = {e^{2x}}\sin x{/tex}
{tex}{a_{22}} = {e^{2x}}.\sin 2.x = {e^{2x}}\sin 2x{/tex}
{tex}{a_{31}} = {e^{3x}}.\sin 1.x = {e^{3x}}\sin x{/tex}
{tex}{a_{32}} = {e^{3x}}.\sin 2x = {e^{3x}}\sin 2x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{e^x}\sin x}&{{e^x}\sin 2x} \\ {{e^{2x}}\sin x}&{{e^{2x}}\sin 2x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{e^{3x}}\sin x}&{{e^{3x}}\sin 2x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find values of a and b if A = B, where {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4}&{3b} \\ 8&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 2}&{{b^2} + 2} \\ 8&{{b^2} - 5b} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
question_html: Find values of a and b if A = B, where {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4}&{3b} \\ 8&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 2}&{{b^2} + 2} \\ 8&{{b^2} - 5b} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4}&{3b} \\ 8&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 2}&{{b^2} + 2} \\ 8&{{b^2} - 5b} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Also, A=B By equality of matrices we know that each element of A is equal to the corresponding element of B, that is {tex}{a_{ij}} = {b_{ij}}{/tex} for all i and j. {tex}{a_{11}} = {b_{11}} \Rightarrow a + 4 = 2a + 2 \Rightarrow a = 2{/tex} {tex}{a_{12}} = {b_{12}} \Rightarrow 3b = {b^2} + 2 \Rightarrow {b^2} = 3b - 2{/tex} And {tex}{a_{22}} = {b_{22}} \Rightarrow - 6 = {b^2} - 5b{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 6 = 3b - 2 - 5b\;\left[ {\because {b^2} = 3b - 2} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2b = 4 \Rightarrow b = 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 2 and b = 2
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4}&{3b} \\ 8&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + 2}&{{b^2} + 2} \\ 8&{{b^2} - 5b} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Also, A=B
By equality of matrices we know that each element of A is equal to the corresponding element of B, that is {tex}{a_{ij}} = {b_{ij}}{/tex} for all i and j.
{tex}{a_{11}} = {b_{11}} \Rightarrow a + 4 = 2a + 2 \Rightarrow a = 2{/tex}
{tex}{a_{12}} = {b_{12}} \Rightarrow 3b = {b^2} + 2 \Rightarrow {b^2} = 3b - 2{/tex}
And {tex}{a_{22}} = {b_{22}} \Rightarrow - 6 = {b^2} - 5b{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 6 = 3b - 2 - 5b\;\left[ {\because {b^2} = 3b - 2} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2b = 4 \Rightarrow b = 2{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 2 and b = 2

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If possible, find the sum of the matrices A and B, where {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\sqrt 3 }&1 \\ 2&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex} B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ a \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y \\ b \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} z \\ c \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
question_html: If possible, find the sum of the matrices A and B, where {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\sqrt 3 }&1 \\ 2&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex} B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ a \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y \\ b \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} z \\ c \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\sqrt 3 }&1 \\ 2&3 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}B = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ a \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y \\ b \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} z \\ c \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} Here, A and B are of different orders. Also, we know that the addition of two matrices A and B is possible only if order of both the matrices A and B should be same. Hence, the sum of matrices A and B is not possible.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\sqrt 3 }&1 \\ 2&3 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex}  and {tex}B = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ a \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} y \\ b \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} z \\ c \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex}
Here, A and B are of different orders. Also, we know that the addition of two matrices A and B is possible only if order of both the matrices A and B should be same.
Hence, the sum of matrices A and B is not possible.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ { - 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ { - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex} Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 7 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then find X + Y 2x - 3Y A matrix Z such that X + Y + Z is a zero matrix.
question_html:
If {tex}X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ { - 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ { - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex} Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 7 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then find

  1. X + Y
  2. 2x - 3Y
  3. A matrix Z such that X + Y + Z is a zero matrix.
solution_text: We have, {tex}X = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ { - 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ { - 3} \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} and {tex}Y = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 7 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 4 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}X + Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3 + 2} \\ {5 + 7} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 1} \\ { - 2 + 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1 - 1} \\ { - 3 + 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5 \\ {12} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 0 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\because 2x = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ { - 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ { - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6 \\ {10} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ { - 4} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ { - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}{2X = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1&{ - 1} \\ 5&{ - 2}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&2&{ - 2} \\ {10}&{ - 4}&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex} {tex}{3Y = 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&{ - 1} \\ 7&2&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&3&{ - 3} \\ {21}&6&{12} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex} {tex}{2X - 3Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6 - 6}&{2 - 3}&{ - 2 + 3} \\ {10 - 21}&{ - 4 - 6}&{ - 6 - 12} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex} {tex}{ = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1}&1 \\ { - 11}&{ - 10}&{ - 18} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex} We need to find matrix Z, such that X + Y + Z is a zero matrix. That is, X + Y + Z = 0 Or, Z = -X – Y Or, Z = -(X + Y) We have already found X + Y in part (i). So, from part (i): {tex}\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {2} & {-2} \\ {12} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Then, Z = - {tex}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {2} & {-2} \\ {12} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Z=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-12} & {0} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathrm{Z}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-12} & {0} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have, {tex}X = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ { - 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ { - 3} \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} and {tex}Y = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 7 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 4 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex}

  1. {tex}X + Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3 + 2} \\ {5 + 7} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 1} \\ { - 2 + 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1 - 1} \\ { - 3 + 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5 \\ {12} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 0 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
  2. {tex}\because 2x = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ { - 2} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ { - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6 \\ {10} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ { - 4} \end{array}\;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2} \\ { - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
    {tex}{2X = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1&{ - 1} \\ 5&{ - 2}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&2&{ - 2} \\ {10}&{ - 4}&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex}
    {tex}{3Y = 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&{ - 1} \\ 7&2&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&3&{ - 3} \\ {21}&6&{12} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex}
    {tex}{2X - 3Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6 - 6}&{2 - 3}&{ - 2 + 3} \\ {10 - 21}&{ - 4 - 6}&{ - 6 - 12} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex} {tex}{ = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1}&1 \\ { - 11}&{ - 10}&{ - 18} \end{array}} \right]}{/tex}

  3. We need to find matrix Z, such that X + Y + Z is a zero matrix.
    That is,
    X + Y + Z = 0
    Or,
    Z = -X – Y
    Or,
    Z = -(X + Y)
    We have already found X + Y in part (i).
    So, from part (i):
    {tex}\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {2} & {-2} \\ {12} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Then,
    Z = -{tex}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {5} & {2} & {-2} \\ {12} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow Z=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-12} & {0} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Thus, {tex}\mathrm{Z}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {-5} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-12} & {0} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find non-zero values of x satisfying the matrix equation: {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&2 \\ 3&x \end{array}} \right] + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{5x} \\ 4&{4x} \end{array}} \right] = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {({x^2} + 8)}&{24} \\ {(10)}&{6x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find non-zero values of x satisfying the matrix equation:{tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&2 \\ 3&x \end{array}} \right] + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{5x} \\ 4&{4x} \end{array}} \right] = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {({x^2} + 8)}&{24} \\ {(10)}&{6x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: Given that, {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&2 \\ 3&x \end{array}} \right] + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{5x} \\ 4&{4x} \end{array}} \right] = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {({x^2} + 8)}&{24} \\ {(10)}&{6x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2}}&{2x} \\ {3x}&{{x^2}} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {16}&{10x} \\ 8&{8x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2} + 16}&{48} \\ {20}&{12x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2} + 16}&{2x + 10x} \\ {3x + 8}&{{x^2} + 8x} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2} + 16}&{48} \\ {20}&{12x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 10x = 48{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 12x = 48{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = \frac{{48}}{{12}} = 4{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that,
{tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&2 \\ 3&x \end{array}} \right] + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{5x} \\ 4&{4x} \end{array}} \right] = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {({x^2} + 8)}&{24} \\ {(10)}&{6x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2}}&{2x} \\ {3x}&{{x^2}} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {16}&{10x} \\ 8&{8x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2} + 16}&{48} \\ {20}&{12x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2} + 16}&{2x + 10x} \\ {3x + 8}&{{x^2} + 8x} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2} + 16}&{48} \\ {20}&{12x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 10x = 48{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 12x = 48{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x = \frac{{48}}{{12}} = 4{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then show that (A + B)(A - B) {tex}\ne{/tex} A 2 - B 2 .
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then show that (A + B)(A - B) {tex}\ne{/tex} A2 - B2.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore (A + B) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 0}&{1 - 1} \\ {1 + 1}&{1 + 0} \end{array}} \right] = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 2&1 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}(A - B) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 - 0}&{1 + 1} \\ {1 - 1}&{1 - 0} \end{array}} \right] = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} Since, (A + B). (A - B) is defined, if the number of columns of (A + B) is equal to the number of rows of (A - B), so here multiplication of matrices (A + B). (A - B) is possible. Now, {tex}{(A + B)_{2 \times 2}} \cdot {(A - B)_{2 \times 2}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 0}&{0 + 0} \\ {0 + 0}&{4 + 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i) Also, A 2 = A.A {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 1}&{0 + 1} \\ {0 + 1}&{1 + 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}{B^2} = B \cdot B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 - 1}&{0 + 0} \\ {0 + 0}&{ - 1 + 0} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {A^2} - {B^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 1&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(ii) Thus, we see that (A + B).(A - B) {tex}\ne{/tex} A 2 - B 2 [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)] {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&5 \end{array}} \right] \ne \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 1&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore (A + B) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 0}&{1 - 1} \\ {1 + 1}&{1 + 0} \end{array}} \right] = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 2&1 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex}
and {tex}(A - B) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 - 0}&{1 + 1} \\ {1 - 1}&{1 - 0} \end{array}} \right] = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex}
Since, (A + B). (A - B) is defined, if the number of columns of (A + B) is equal to the number of rows of (A - B), so here multiplication of matrices (A + B). (A - B) is possible.
Now, {tex}{(A + B)_{2 \times 2}} \cdot {(A - B)_{2 \times 2}} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 0}&{0 + 0} \\ {0 + 0}&{4 + 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
Also, A2 = A.A
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 1}&{0 + 1} \\ {0 + 1}&{1 + 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}{B^2} = B \cdot B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 1} \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 - 1}&{0 + 0} \\ {0 + 0}&{ - 1 + 0} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {A^2} - {B^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 1&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(ii)
Thus, we see that
(A + B).(A - B) {tex}\ne{/tex} A2 - B2 [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&5 \end{array}} \right] \ne \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 1&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the value of x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
question_html: Find the value of x, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 2x + 15}&{3 + 5x + 3}&{2 + x + 2} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {16 + 2x}&{5x + 6}&{x + 4} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {16 + 2x + (5x + 6).2 + (x + 4)x} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [16 + 2x + 10x + 12 + x 2 + 4x] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 + 16x + 28] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x 2 + 2x + 14x + 28] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x + 14) = 0 {tex}\therefore {/tex} x = -2, -14
solution_html:
We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&5&1 \\ {15}&3&2 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 2x + 15}&{3 + 5x + 3}&{2 + x + 2} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{/tex}{tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {16 + 2x}&{5x + 6}&{x + 4} \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 2 \\ x \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {\left[ {16 + 2x + (5x + 6).2 + (x + 4)x} \right]_{1 \times 1}} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [16 + 2x + 10x + 12 + x2 + 4x] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x2 + 16x + 28] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x2 + 2x + 14x + 28] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 2)(x + 14) = 0
{tex}\therefore {/tex} x = -2, -14

question_text: Show that A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} satisfies the equation A 2 – 3A – 7I = O and hence find A –1
question_html: Show that A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} satisfies the equation A2 – 3A – 7I = O and hence find A–1
solution_text: We have A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 = A. A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 25-3 & 15-6 \\ -5+2 & -3+4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 22 & 9 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 3A = 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 15 & 9 \\ -3 & -6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and 7I 2 = 7 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 - 3A - 7 I 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 22 & 9 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 15 & 9 \\ -3 & -6 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 22-15-7 & 9-9-0 \\ -3+3-0 & 1+6-7 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]=0{/tex} Now we have, A 2 - 3A - 7I = 0 2 Pre operating both sides with A -1 , we get A -1 [A 2 - 3A - 7 I 2 ] = A -1 O 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A −1 ⋅A ⋅ A −3 A −1 ⋅ A−7 A −1 ⋅ I = O 2 Since, A −1 ⋅A = I 2 , we get the following equation {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I . A - 3 I 2 - 7A -1 = O 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} A -1 . A = I] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A - 3 I 2 - 7A -1 = O 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7A -1 = A - 3 I 2 {tex}7 A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 3 \\ -1 & -5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad A^{-1}=\frac{1}{7}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 3 \\ -1 & -5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 = A. A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 25-3 & 15-6 \\ -5+2 & -3+4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 22 & 9 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
3A = 3{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 15 & 9 \\ -3 & -6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and 7I 2 = 7{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 - 3A - 7 I2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 22 & 9 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} 15 & 9 \\ -3 & -6 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 22-15-7 & 9-9-0 \\ -3+3-0 & 1+6-7 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]=0{/tex}
Now we have,
A2 - 3A - 7I = 02
Pre operating  both sides with A-1, we get
A-1 [A2 - 3A - 7 I2] = A-1 O2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A−1⋅A ⋅ A−3 A−1⋅ A−7 A−1 ⋅ I = O2

Since, A−1⋅A = I2 , we get the following equation
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I . A - 3 I2 - 7A-1 = O2 [{tex}\because{/tex} A-1 . A = I]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A - 3 I2 - 7A-1 = O2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  7A-1 = A - 3 I2
{tex}7 A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 3 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 3 \\ -1 & -5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad A^{-1}=\frac{1}{7}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 3 \\ -1 & -5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the matrix A satisfying the matrix equation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right] \text { A }\left[\begin{array}{cc} -3 & 2 \\ 5 & -3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find the matrix A satisfying the matrix equation:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right] \text { A }\left[\begin{array}{cc} -3 & 2 \\ 5 & -3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 3&2 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}A{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&2 \\ 5&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}} = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} Let {tex}\begin{array}{l}A=\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}\\\end{array}{/tex} ,since all the orders of the matrix in product are 2x2, we assume the matrix A as 2x2 matrix. Hence the given equation can be modified as , {tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 3&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&2 \\ 5&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + c}&{2b + d} \\ {3a + 2c}&{3b + 2d} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&2 \\ 5&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 6a - 3c + 10b + 5d}&{4a + 2c - 6b - 3d} \\ { - 9a + 6c + 15b + 10d}&{6a + 4c - 9b - 6d} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating the corresponding elements of the matrices on the both sides of the equation, we have the following equations. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -6a - 3c + 10b + 5d = 1 ....(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4a + 2c - 6b - 3d = 0 ....(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -9a - 6c + 15b + 10d = 0 ....(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6a + 4c - 9b - 6d = 1 .......(iv) On adding Eqs. (i) and (iv), we get c + b - d = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = c + b - 2 ....(v) On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get -5a - 4c + 9b + 7d = 0 ....(vi) On adding Eqs. (vi) and (iv), we get a + 0 + 0 + d = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 1 - a ....(vii) From Eqs. (v) and (vii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c + b - 2 = 1 - a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b + c = 3 ....(viii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3 - b - c Now, using the values of a and d in Eq. (iii) , we get -9(3 - b - c) - 6c + 15b + 10(-2 + b + c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -27 + 9b + 9c - 6c + 15b - 20 + 10b + 10c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 34b + 13c = 47 Now, using the values of a and d in Eq. (ii), we get 4(3 - b - c) + 2c - 6b - 3(b + c - 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12 - 4b - 4c + 2c - 6b - 3b - 3c + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -13b + 5c = 18 ....(x) On multiplying Eq. (ix) by 5 and Eq. (x) by 13, then adding, we get {tex}\frac{{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 169b - 65c = - 234} \\ {170b + 65c = 235} \end{array}}}{{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;b = 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -13 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 5c = -18 [from Eq. (x)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -5c = -18 + 13 = -5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1 a = 3 - 1 - 1 = 1 and d = 1 - 1 = 0 {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 3&2 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}A{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&2 \\ 5&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}} = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex}
Let {tex}\begin{array}{l}A=\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}\\\end{array}{/tex} ,since all the orders of the matrix in product are 2x2, we assume the matrix A as 2x2 matrix.

Hence the given equation can be modified as ,
{tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 3&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&2 \\ 5&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a + c}&{2b + d} \\ {3a + 2c}&{3b + 2d} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&2 \\ 5&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 6a - 3c + 10b + 5d}&{4a + 2c - 6b - 3d} \\ { - 9a + 6c + 15b + 10d}&{6a + 4c - 9b - 6d} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

Equating the corresponding elements of the matrices on the both sides of the equation, we have the following equations.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -6a - 3c + 10b + 5d = 1 ....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4a + 2c - 6b - 3d = 0 ....(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -9a - 6c + 15b + 10d = 0 ....(iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6a + 4c - 9b - 6d = 1 .......(iv)
On adding Eqs. (i) and (iv), we get
c + b - d = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = c + b - 2 ....(v)
On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get
-5a - 4c + 9b + 7d = 0 ....(vi)
On adding Eqs. (vi) and (iv), we get
a + 0 + 0 + d = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 1 - a ....(vii)
From Eqs. (v) and (vii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c + b - 2 = 1 - a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b + c = 3 ....(viii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3 - b - c
Now, using the values of a and d in Eq. (iii) , we get
-9(3 - b - c) - 6c + 15b + 10(-2 + b + c) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -27 + 9b + 9c - 6c + 15b - 20 + 10b + 10c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 34b + 13c = 47
Now, using the values of a and d in Eq. (ii), we get
4(3 - b - c) + 2c - 6b - 3(b + c - 2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12 - 4b - 4c + 2c - 6b - 3b - 3c + 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -13b + 5c = 18 ....(x)
On multiplying Eq. (ix) by 5 and Eq. (x) by 13, then adding, we get
{tex}\frac{{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 169b - 65c = - 234} \\ {170b + 65c = 235} \end{array}}}{{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;b = 1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -13 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 - 5c = -18 [from Eq. (x)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -5c = -18 + 13 = -5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
a = 3 - 1 - 1 = 1 and d = 1 - 1 = 0
{tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find A, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find A, if {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{/tex} Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y&z \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}} = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {4x}&{4y}&{4z} \\ x&y&z \\ {3x}&{3y}&{3z} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 4x = - 4 \Rightarrow x = - 1,4y = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 and 4z = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 1 {tex}\therefore A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{/tex}
Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 1}}{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&y&z \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 3}} = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]_{3 \times 3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {4x}&{4y}&{4z} \\ x&y&z \\ {3x}&{3y}&{3z} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&8&4 \\ { - 1}&2&1 \\ { - 3}&6&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 4x = - 4 \Rightarrow x = - 1,4y = 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 and 4z = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 1
{tex}\therefore A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 4} \\ 1&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} , then verify {tex}{(BA)^2} \ne {B^2}{A^2}{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 4} \\ 1&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}, then verify {tex}{(BA)^2} \ne {B^2}{A^2}{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 4} \\ 1&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore BA = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 4} \\ 1&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6 + 1 + 4}&{ - 8 + 1 + 0} \\ {3 + 2 + 8}&{ - 4 + 2 + 0} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {11}&{ - 7} \\ {13}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}(BA).(BA) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {11}&{ - 7} \\ {13}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {11}&{ - 7} \\ {13}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {(BA)^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {121 - 91}&{ - 77 + 14} \\ {143 - 26}&{ - 91 + 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30}&{ - 63} \\ {117}&{ - 87} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}{B^2} = B.B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} So, B 2 is not possible, since B is not a square matrix. Hence, {tex}{(BA)^2} \ne {B^2}{A^2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 4} \\ 1&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore BA = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 4} \\ 1&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {6 + 1 + 4}&{ - 8 + 1 + 0} \\ {3 + 2 + 8}&{ - 4 + 2 + 0} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {11}&{ - 7} \\ {13}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}(BA).(BA) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {11}&{ - 7} \\ {13}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {11}&{ - 7} \\ {13}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {(BA)^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {121 - 91}&{ - 77 + 14} \\ {143 - 26}&{ - 91 + 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30}&{ - 63} \\ {117}&{ - 87} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i)
Also, {tex}{B^2} = B.B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex}
So, B2 is not possible, since B is not a square matrix.
Hence, {tex}{(BA)^2} \ne {B^2}{A^2}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If possible, find the value of BA and AB, where {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
question_html: If possible, find the value of BA and AB, where {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex} So, AB and BA both are possible. [Since, in both AB and BA, the number of columns of first is equal to the number of rows of second.] {tex}\begin{array}{l}AB=\begin{bmatrix}2&1&2\\1&2&4\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}4&1\\2&3\\1&2\end{bmatrix}\\\end{array}{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 2 + 2}&{2 + 3 + 4} \\ {4 + 4 + 4}&{1 + 6 + 8} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {12}&9 \\ {12}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}BA = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {4 \times 2 + 1}&{4 + 2}&{8 + 4} \\ {4 + 3}&{2 + 6}&{4 + 12} \\ {2 + 2}&{1 + 4}&{2 + 8} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&6&{12} \\ 7&8&{16} \\ 4&5&{10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex}
So, AB and BA both are possible.
[Since, in both AB and BA, the number of columns of first is equal to the number of rows of second.]
{tex}\begin{array}{l}AB=\begin{bmatrix}2&1&2\\1&2&4\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}4&1\\2&3\\1&2\end{bmatrix}\\\end{array}{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 2 + 2}&{2 + 3 + 4} \\ {4 + 4 + 4}&{1 + 6 + 8} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {12}&9 \\ {12}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}BA = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {4 \times 2 + 1}&{4 + 2}&{8 + 4} \\ {4 + 3}&{2 + 6}&{4 + 12} \\ {2 + 2}&{1 + 4}&{2 + 8} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&6&{12} \\ 7&8&{16} \\ 4&5&{10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Show by an example that for {tex}A \ne O,\;B \ne O{/tex} , AB = O.
question_html: Show by an example that for {tex}A \ne O,\;B \ne O{/tex}, AB = O.
solution_text: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 4} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right] \ne 0{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] \ne 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 4} \\ 0&2 \end{array}} \right] \ne 0{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] \ne 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Given {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&9&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&8 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} . Is (AB)′= B′A′?
question_html: Given {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&9&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&8 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}. Is (AB)′= B′A′?
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&9&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} and {tex}B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&8 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 8 + 0}&{8 + 32 + 0} \\ {3 + 18 + 6}&{12 + 72 + 18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&{40} \\ {27}&{102} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}(AB)' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&{27} \\ {40}&{102} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i) Also, {tex}B' = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&8&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} and {tex}A' = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&9 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore B'A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 8 + 0}&{3 + 18 + 6} \\ {8 + 32 + 0}&{12 + 72 + 18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&{27} \\ {40}&{102} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(ii) Thus, we see that, (AB)’ = B’A’ [From Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&0 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&9&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} and {tex}B = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&8 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 8 + 0}&{8 + 32 + 0} \\ {3 + 18 + 6}&{12 + 72 + 18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&{40} \\ {27}&{102} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}(AB)' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&{27} \\ {40}&{102} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
Also, {tex}B' = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&8&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{2 \times 3}}{/tex} and {tex}A' = {\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&9 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]_{3 \times 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore B'A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 8 + 0}&{3 + 18 + 6} \\ {8 + 32 + 0}&{12 + 72 + 18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10}&{27} \\ {40}&{102} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(ii)
Thus, we see that, (AB)’ = B’A’ [From Eqs. (i) and (ii)]

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Solve for x and y, {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ { - 11} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
question_html: Solve for x and y, {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ { - 11} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
solution_text: we have, {tex}x\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\end{bmatrix}+y\begin{bmatrix}3\\5\end{bmatrix}+\begin{bmatrix}-8\\-11\end{bmatrix}=0{/tex} {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ { - 11} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x} \\ x \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3.y} \\ {5.y} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ { - 11} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}2x+3y-8\\x+5y-11\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x + 3y - 8 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x + 6y = 16 ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x + 20y = 44 On subtracting Eq. (i) and from (ii), we get 14y = 28 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 {tex}\therefore 2x + 3 \times 2 - 8 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 1 and y = 2
solution_html:
we have, {tex}x\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\end{bmatrix}+y\begin{bmatrix}3\\5\end{bmatrix}+\begin{bmatrix}-8\\-11\end{bmatrix}=0{/tex}
{tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2 \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3 \\ 5 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ { - 11} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x} \\ x \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3.y} \\ {5.y} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8} \\ { - 11} \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\begin{bmatrix}2x+3y-8\\x+5y-11\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x + 3y - 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x + 6y = 16 ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x + 20y = 44
On subtracting Eq. (i) and from (ii), we get
14y = 28 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2
{tex}\therefore 2x + 3 \times 2 - 8 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 1 and y = 2

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If X and Y are {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} matrices, then solve the following matrix equations for X and Y {tex}2X + 3Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right],\;3X + 2Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&2 \\ 1&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
question_html:
If X and Y are {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} matrices, then solve the following matrix equations for X and Y
{tex}2X + 3Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right],\;3X + 2Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&2 \\ 1&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}2X + 3Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i) And {tex}3X + 2Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&2 \\ 1&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) On subtracting Eq. (i) from Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\therefore (3X + 2Y) - (2X + 3Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2 - 2}&{2 - 3} \\ {1 - 4}&{ - 5 - 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}(X - Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&{ - 1} \\ { - 3}&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(iii) On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get {tex}(5X + 5Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&5 \\ 5&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (X + Y) = \frac{1}{5}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&5 \\ 5&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(iv) On adding Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}\Rightarrow (X - Y)(X + Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&0 \\ { - 2}&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2X = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} From Eq. (iv), {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] + Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 2}&{1 - 0} \\ {1 + 1}&{ - 1 + 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 2&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}2X + 3Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
And {tex}3X + 2Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&2 \\ 1&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii)
On subtracting Eq. (i) from Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\therefore (3X + 2Y) - (2X + 3Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2 - 2}&{2 - 3} \\ {1 - 4}&{ - 5 - 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}(X - Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&{ - 1} \\ { - 3}&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(iii)
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}(5X + 5Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&5 \\ 5&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow (X + Y) = \frac{1}{5}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&5 \\ 5&{ - 5} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(iv)
On adding Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow (X - Y)(X + Y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 4}&0 \\ { - 2}&{ - 6} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2X = 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore X = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
From Eq. (iv),
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right] + Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;Y = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + 2}&{1 - 0} \\ {1 + 1}&{ - 1 + 3} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \\ 2&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then find a non-zero matrix C such that AC = BC.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then find a non-zero matrix C such that AC = BC.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}B = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 2}}{/tex} Let {tex}C = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 1}}{/tex} is a non-zero matrix of order {tex}2 \times 1{/tex} . {tex}\therefore AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {3x + 5y} \right]{/tex} And {tex}BC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {7x + 3y} \right]{/tex} For AC=BC, [3x + 5y] = [7x + 3y] On using equality of matrix, we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 5y = 7x + 3y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x = 2y {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{2}y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = 2x{/tex} {tex}\therefore C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ {2x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} We see that on taking C of order {tex}2 \times 1,\;2 \times 2,\;2 \times 3,\;....{/tex} we get {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ {2x} \end{array}} \right],\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&x \\ {2x}&{2x} \end{array}} \right],\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;x}&{\;x}&{\;x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&{2x}&{2x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right],....{/tex} In general, {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} k \\ {2k} \end{array}} \right],\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} k&k \\ {2k}&{2k} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , ...... Where, k is any real number.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 2}}{/tex} and {tex}B = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \end{array}} \right]_{1 \times 2}}{/tex}
Let {tex}C = {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \end{array}} \right]_{2 \times 1}}{/tex} is a non-zero matrix of order {tex}2 \times 1{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&5 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {3x + 5y} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}BC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ y \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {7x + 3y} \right]{/tex}
For AC=BC,
[3x + 5y] = [7x + 3y]
On using equality of matrix, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 5y = 7x + 3y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x = 2y
{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{1}{2}y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y = 2x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ {2x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
We see that on taking C of order {tex}2 \times 1,\;2 \times 2,\;2 \times 3,\;....{/tex} we get
{tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ {2x} \end{array}} \right],\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&x \\ {2x}&{2x} \end{array}} \right],\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;x}&{\;x}&{\;x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&{2x}&{2x} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right],....{/tex}
In general,
{tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} k \\ {2k} \end{array}} \right],\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} k&k \\ {2k}&{2k} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, ......
Where, k is any real number.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Give an example of matrices A, B and C such that AB = AC, where A is non-zero matrix, but B ≠ C.
question_html: Give an example of matrices A, B and C such that AB = AC, where A is non-zero matrix, but B ≠ C.
solution_text: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\because B \ne C} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i) And {tex}\therefore AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .....(ii) Thus, we see that AB = AC [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)] Where, A is non-zero matrix but {tex}B \ne C{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&4 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\because B \ne C} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
And {tex}\therefore AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 4&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .....(ii)
Thus, we see that AB = AC [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
Where, A is non-zero matrix but {tex}B \ne C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , verify (AB)C = A(BC)
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, verify (AB)C = A(BC)
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left( {AB} \right) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 6}&{3 - 8} \\ { - 4 + 3}&{ - 6 - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}(AB)C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 5}&0 \\ { - 1 + 10}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {13}&0 \\ 9&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) Again, {tex}{BC} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 - 3}&0 \\ {3 + 4}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 7&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex} A(BC) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 7&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1 + 14}&0 \\ { + 2 + 7}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {13}&0 \\ 9&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} (AB)C = A(BC) [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\left( {AB} \right) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 6}&{3 - 8} \\ { - 4 + 3}&{ - 6 - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}(AB)C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 5}&0 \\ { - 1 + 10}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {13}&0 \\ 9&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i)
Again, {tex}{BC} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 - 3}&0 \\ {3 + 4}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 7&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex} A(BC) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ 7&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1 + 14}&0 \\ { + 2 + 7}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {13}&0 \\ 9&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (AB)C = A(BC) [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , verify A(B + C) = AB + AC
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, verify A(B + C) = AB + AC
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}(B + C) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&3 \\ 2&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}A(B + C) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&3 \\ 2&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3 + 4}&{3 - 8} \\ { - 6 + 2}&{ - 6 - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(iii) Also, {tex}AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 6}&{3 - 8} \\ { - 4 + 3}&{ - 6 - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - 2}&0 \\ { - 2 - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore AB + AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow AB + AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(iv) From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), A(B + C) = AB + AC
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right],B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}(B + C) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&3 \\ 2&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}A(B + C) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&3 \\ 2&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3 + 4}&{3 - 8} \\ { - 6 + 2}&{ - 6 - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(iii)
Also, {tex}AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 3&{ - 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 + 6}&{3 - 8} \\ { - 4 + 3}&{ - 6 - 4} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ { - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - 2}&0 \\ { - 2 - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore AB + AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&{ - 5} \\ { - 1}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow AB + AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&{ - 10} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(iv)
From Eqs. (iii) and (iv),
A(B + C) = AB + AC

question_text: If {tex}P = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&0&0 \\ 0&y&0 \\ 0&0&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0 \\ 0&b&0 \\ 0&0&c \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then prove that {tex}PQ = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xa}&0&0 \\ 0&{yb}&0 \\ 0&0&{zc} \end{array}} \right] = QP{/tex} . .
question_html:
If {tex}P = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&0&0 \\ 0&y&0 \\ 0&0&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}Q = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0 \\ 0&b&0 \\ 0&0&c \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then prove that {tex}PQ = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xa}&0&0 \\ 0&{yb}&0 \\ 0&0&{zc} \end{array}} \right] = QP{/tex}.
.
solution_text: {tex}PQ = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&0&0 \\ 0&y&0 \\ 0&0&z \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0 \\ 0&b&0 \\ 0&0&c \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xa}&0&0 \\ 0&{yb}&0 \\ 0&0&{zc} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i) and {tex}QP = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0 \\ 0&b&0 \\ 0&0&c \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&0&0 \\ 0&y&0 \\ 0&0&z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {ax}&0&0 \\ 0&{by}&0 \\ 0&0&{cz} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) Thus, we see that, PQ=QP [using Eqs. (i) and (ii) ] Hence proved.
solution_html:
{tex}PQ = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&0&0 \\ 0&y&0 \\ 0&0&z \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0 \\ 0&b&0 \\ 0&0&c \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xa}&0&0 \\ 0&{yb}&0 \\ 0&0&{zc} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
and {tex}QP = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&0&0 \\ 0&b&0 \\ 0&0&c \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&0&0 \\ 0&y&0 \\ 0&0&z \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {ax}&0&0 \\ 0&{by}&0 \\ 0&0&{cz} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii)
Thus, we see that, PQ=QP [using Eqs. (i) and (ii) ]
Hence proved.
