book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 1} \\ { - 1}&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right] = A{/tex} , then find the value of A.
question_html: If {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 1} \\ { - 1}&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right] = A{/tex}, then find the value of A.
solution_text: We have, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 1} \\ { - 1}&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right] = A{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 1} \\ { - 1}&1&0 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2 - 1 + 0}&{0 + 1 + 3}&{ - 2 + 0 + 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&4&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right] = A{/tex} {tex}\therefore A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&4&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = [ - 3 + 0 - 1] = [ -4 ]
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 1} \\ { - 1}&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right] = A{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 1} \\ { - 1}&1&0 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2 - 1 + 0}&{0 + 1 + 3}&{ - 2 + 0 + 3} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&4&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right] = A{/tex}
{tex}\therefore A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&4&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1 \\ 0 \\ { - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
= [ - 3 + 0 - 1] = [ -4 ]

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&3&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&7&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\;\;0}&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} , then verify that A(B + C) = (AB + AC).
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&3&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&7&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\;\;0}&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}, then verify that A(B + C) = (AB + AC).
solution_text: We have to verify that, A(B + C) = (AB + AC) We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&3&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&7&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\;\;0}&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore A(B + C) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {5 - 1}&{3 + 2}&{4 + 1} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 1}&{7 + 0}&{6 + 2} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&5&5 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&7&8 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 9}&{10 + 7}&{10 + 8} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {17}&{17}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i) Also, {tex}AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&3&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&7&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} ....(i) {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10 + 8}&{6 + 7}&{8 + 6} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {18}&{13}&{14} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;1}&0&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2 + 1}&{4 + 0}&{2 + 2} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&4&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore AB + AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {18}&{13}&{14} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&4&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {17}&{17}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\therefore A(B + C) = (AB + AC){/tex} [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)] Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have to verify that, A(B + C) = (AB + AC)

We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&3&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&7&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} and {tex}C = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{\;\;0}&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore A(B + C) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {5 - 1}&{3 + 2}&{4 + 1} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 1}&{7 + 0}&{6 + 2} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&5&5 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&7&8 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 + 9}&{10 + 7}&{10 + 8} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {17}&{17}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
Also, {tex}AB = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&3&4 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&7&6 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {10 + 8}&{6 + 7}&{8 + 6} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {18}&{13}&{14} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&2&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;1}&0&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 2 + 1}&{4 + 0}&{2 + 2} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&4&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore AB + AC = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {18}&{13}&{14} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&4&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {17}&{17}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore A(B + C) = (AB + AC){/tex} [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then verify that A 2 + A = A(A + I), where I is {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} unit matrix.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then verify that A2 + A = A(A + I), where I is {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} unit matrix.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {A^2} = A.A{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \\ 4&4&4 \\ 2&2&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {A^2} + A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \\ 4&4&4 \\ 2&2&4 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&{ - 3} \\ 6&5&7 \\ 2&3&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}A + I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&2&3 \\ 0&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}A(A + I) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&2&3 \\ 0&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&{ - 3} \\ 6&5&7 \\ 2&3&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) Thus, we see that A 2 + A = A(A + I) [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {A^2} = A.A{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \\ 4&4&4 \\ 2&2&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {A^2} + A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \\ 4&4&4 \\ 2&2&4 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&{ - 3} \\ 6&5&7 \\ 2&3&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i)
Now, {tex}A + I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&2&3 \\ 0&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}A(A + I) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&1&3 \\ 0&1&1 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 2&2&3 \\ 0&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&{ - 3} \\ 6&5&7 \\ 2&3&5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii)
Thus, we see that A2 + A = A(A + I) [using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]

question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that:(A’)’ = A
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that:(A’)’ = A
solution_text: We are given with matrices A, such that {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to verify that, (A’)’ = A. Take L.H.S: (A’)’ If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , Then {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Also, if {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , Then {tex}\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Note, that {tex}\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex} Thus, verified that (A’)’ = A.
solution_html:
We are given with matrices A, such that
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We need to verify that, (A’)’ = A.
Take L.H.S: (A’)’
If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex},
Then {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Also, if {tex}A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex},
Then {tex}\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Note, that {tex}\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex}
Thus, verified that (A’)’ = A.

question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} \\ {2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that: (AB)′ {tex}={/tex} B′A′
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} \\ {2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that: (AB)′ {tex}={/tex} B′A′
solution_text: We are given with matrices A and B, such that {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} \\ {2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}(AB)=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {10} \\ {12} & {-6} \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow (AB)'=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {12} \\ {10} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} also, B'A' {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {12} \\ {10} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (AB)' Hence proved.
solution_html:
We are given with matrices A and B, such that
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {3} \\ {2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}(AB)=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {10} \\ {12} & {-6} \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow (AB)'=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {12} \\ {10} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
also, B'A'  {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {2} & {12} \\ {10} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}= (AB)'
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that: (kA)′ = (kA′).
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that: (kA)′ = (kA′).
solution_text: We need to verify that, (kA)’ = kA’. Take L.H.S: (kA)’ We know that, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Multiply k on both sides, (k is a scalar quantity) {tex}\mathrm{kA}=\mathrm{k} \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {\mathrm{k} \times 0} & {\mathrm{k} \times-1} & {\mathrm{k} \times 2} \\ {\mathrm{k} \times 4} & {\mathrm{k} \times 3} & {\mathrm{k} \times-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-\mathrm{k}} & {2 \mathrm{k}} \\ {4 \mathrm{k}} & {3 \mathrm{k}} & {-4 \mathrm{k}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\mathrm{kA})^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4 \mathrm{k}} \\ {-\mathrm{k}} & {3 \mathrm{k}} \\ {2 \mathrm{k}} & {-4 \mathrm{k}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Take R.H.S: kA’ If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Multiply k on both sides, {tex}\mathrm{kA}^{\prime}=\mathrm{k}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {\mathrm{k} \times 0} & {\mathrm{k} \times 4} \\ {\mathrm{k} \times-1} & {\mathrm{k} \times 3} \\ {\mathrm{k} \times 2} & {\mathrm{k} \times-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4 \mathrm{k}} \\ {-\mathrm{k}} & {3 \mathrm{k}} \\ {2 \mathrm{k}} & {-4 \mathrm{k}} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Note that, L.H.S = R.H.S. Thus, (kA)’ = kA’.
solution_html:
We need to verify that, (kA)’ = kA’.
Take L.H.S: (kA)’
We know that,
{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Multiply k on both sides, (k is a scalar quantity)
{tex}\mathrm{kA}=\mathrm{k} \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {\mathrm{k} \times 0} & {\mathrm{k} \times-1} & {\mathrm{k} \times 2} \\ {\mathrm{k} \times 4} & {\mathrm{k} \times 3} & {\mathrm{k} \times-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-\mathrm{k}} & {2 \mathrm{k}} \\ {4 \mathrm{k}} & {3 \mathrm{k}} & {-4 \mathrm{k}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\mathrm{kA})^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4 \mathrm{k}} \\ {-\mathrm{k}} & {3 \mathrm{k}} \\ {2 \mathrm{k}} & {-4 \mathrm{k}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Take R.H.S: kA’
If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {3} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Multiply k on both sides,
{tex}\mathrm{kA}^{\prime}=\mathrm{k}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {3} \\ {2} & {-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {\mathrm{k} \times 0} & {\mathrm{k} \times 4} \\ {\mathrm{k} \times-1} & {\mathrm{k} \times 3} \\ {\mathrm{k} \times 2} & {\mathrm{k} \times-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {4 \mathrm{k}} \\ {-\mathrm{k}} & {3 \mathrm{k}} \\ {2 \mathrm{k}} & {-4 \mathrm{k}} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Note that, L.H.S = R.H.S.
Thus, (kA)’ = kA’.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that: (2A + B)′ = 2A′ + B′
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}  then verify that: (2A + B)′ = 2A′ + B′
solution_text: We have, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} (2A + B) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {4} \\ {8} & {2} \\ {10} & {12} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {6} \\ {14} & {6} \\ {17} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and (2A + B)' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {14} & {17} \\ {6} & {6} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Also, 2A' + B' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {8} & {10} \\ {4} & {2} & {12} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {6} & {7} \\ {2} & {4} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {14} & {17} \\ {6} & {6} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (2A + B)' So, 2A' + B' = (2A + B)'
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
(2A + B) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {4} \\ {8} & {2} \\ {10} & {12} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {3} & {6} \\ {14} & {6} \\ {17} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and (2A + B)' = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {3} & {14} & {17} \\ {6} & {6} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Also, 2A' + B'
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {8} & {10} \\ {4} & {2} & {12} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {6} & {7} \\ {2} & {4} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {14} & {17} \\ {6} & {6} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (2A + B)'
So, 2A' + B' = (2A + B)'

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then verify that: (A – B)′ = A′ – B′.
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}  then verify that: (A – B)′ = A′ – B′.
solution_text: {tex}A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {0} \\ {-2} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}(A-B)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {0} & {-3} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} also, {tex}A^{\prime}-B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {6} & {7} \\ {2} & {4} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {0} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {0} & {-3} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (A - B)' So, hence proved A' - B' = (A - B)'
solution_html:
{tex}A-B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {4} & {1} \\ {5} & {6} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {2} \\ {6} & {4} \\ {7} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {0} & {0} \\ {-2} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and {tex}(A-B)^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {0} & {-3} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
also, {tex}A^{\prime}-B^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {4} & {5} \\ {2} & {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {6} & {7} \\ {2} & {4} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {0} & {-2} & {-2} \\ {0} & {-3} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (A - B)'
So, hence proved A' - B' = (A - B)'

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Show that A′A and AA′ are both symmetric matrices for any matrix A.
question_html: Show that A′A and AA′ are both symmetric matrices for any matrix A.
solution_text: Let P = A’A P' = (A'A)' = A'(A')'[as (AB)' = B'A'] = A'A = P So, A’A is symmetric matrix for any matrix A. Similarly, let Q = AA’ {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q' = (AA')' = (A')'(A)' = AA' = Q So, AA’ is symmetric matrix for any matrix A.
solution_html:
Let P = A’A
P' = (A'A)'
= A'(A')'[as (AB)' = B'A']
= A'A = P
So, A’A is symmetric matrix for any matrix A.
Similarly, let Q = AA’
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Q' = (AA')' = (A')'(A)'
= AA' = Q
So, AA’ is symmetric matrix for any matrix A.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A and B be square matrices of the order 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 3. Is (AB) 2 = A 2 B 2 ? Give reasons.
question_html: Let A and B be square matrices of the order 3{tex} \times {/tex}3. Is (AB)2 = A2B2? Give reasons.
solution_text: Since, A and B are square matrices of order {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} , there {tex}A^2,B^2,(AB)^2 {/tex} are defined and AB = BA {tex}\therefore {/tex} (AB) 2 = (AB)(AB) = ABAB = AABB {tex}\left[ {\because AB = BA} \right]{/tex} = A 2 B 2 So, AB 2 = A 2 B 2 is true when AB = BA
solution_html:
Since, A and B are square matrices of order {tex}3 \times 3{/tex}, there {tex}A^2,B^2,(AB)^2 {/tex} are defined and AB = BA
{tex}\therefore {/tex} (AB)2 = (AB)(AB)
= ABAB
= AABB {tex}\left[ {\because AB = BA} \right]{/tex}
= A2B2
So, AB2 = A2B2 is true when AB = BA

question_text: Show that if A and B are square matrices such that AB = BA, then (A + B) 2 = A 2 + 2AB + B 2
question_html: Show that if A and B are square matrices such that AB = BA, then (A + B)2 = A2 + 2AB + B2
solution_text: Since, A and B are square matrices such that AB = BA {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A + B) 2 = (A + B).(A + B) = A 2 + AB + BA + B 2 = A 2 + AB + AB + B 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} AB = BA] = A 2 + 2AB + B 2 Hence proved.
solution_html:
Since, A and B are square matrices such that AB = BA
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (A + B)2 = (A + B).(A + B)
= A2 + AB + BA + B2
= A2 + AB + AB + B2 [{tex}\because{/tex} AB = BA]
= A2 + 2AB + B2

Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], ~\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Show that A + (B + C) = (A + B) + C
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], ~\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Show that A + (B + C) = (A + B) + C
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} A + (B + C) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {6} & {0} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {2} \\ {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and (A + B) + C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {2} \\ {0} & {8} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {2} \\ {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A + (B + C)
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
A + (B + C) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ll} {6} & {0} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {2} \\ {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and (A + B) + C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {5} & {2} \\ {0} & {8} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {7} & {2} \\ {1} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}= A + (B + C)

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], ~\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Show that A (BC) = (AB) C
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], ~\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
Show that A (BC) = (AB) C
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} (BC) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {7} & {-10} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}A(B C)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {7} & {-10} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8+14} & {0-20} \\ {-8+21} & {0-30} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {22} & {-20} \\ {13} & {-30} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Also, AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4+2} & {0+10} \\ {-4+3} & {0+15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {10} \\ {-1} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(A B) C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {10} \\ {-1} & {15} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {22} & {-20} \\ {13} & {-30} \end{array}\right]=A(B C){/tex} Hnece proved.
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
(BC) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {7} & {-10} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and {tex}A(B C)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {7} & {-10} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8+14} & {0-20} \\ {-8+21} & {0-30} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {22} & {-20} \\ {13} & {-30} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Also, AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4+2} & {0+10} \\ {-4+3} & {0+15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {10} \\ {-1} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(A B) C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {10} \\ {-1} & {15} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {22} & {-20} \\ {13} & {-30} \end{array}\right]=A(B C){/tex}
Hnece proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right],\quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and a = 4, b = –2. Show that (a + b)B = aB + bB
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right],\quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and a = 4, b = –2.
Show that (a + b)B = aB + bB
solution_text: (a + b) B = (4 - 2) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} given a = 4, b = - 2] = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {2} & {10} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Also, aB + bB = 4B - 2B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {16} & {0} \\ {4} & {20} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {2} & {10} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {2} & {10} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (a + b) B Hence proved
solution_html:
(a + b) B = (4 - 2) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} given a = 4, b = - 2]
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {2} & {10} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Also, aB + bB
= 4B - 2B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {16} & {0} \\ {4} & {20} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {2} & {10} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {2} & {10} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (a + b) B
Hence proved

question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and a = 4. Show that a(C–A) = aC – aA
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], ~ \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and a = 4.
Show that a(C–A) = aC – aA
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and a = 4, C - A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-2} \\ {2} & {-5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and a(C - A) = 4(C - A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {-8} \\ {8} & {-20} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Also, aC - aA = 4C - 4A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {4} & {-8} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {8} \\ {-4} & {12} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {-8} \\ {8} & {-20} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = a(C - A) Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and a = 4,
C - A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-2} \\ {2} & {-5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and a(C - A) = 4(C - A)
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {-8} \\ {8} & {-20} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Also, aC - aA = 4C - 4A
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {4} & {-8} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {8} \\ {-4} & {12} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {-8} \\ {8} & {-20} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = a(C - A)
Hence proved.

question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Show that (A T ) T = A
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Show that (AT)T = A
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, {tex}A^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left(A^{T}\right)^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}A^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left(A^{T}\right)^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=A{/tex}
Hence proved

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and b = –2. Show that (bA) T = b A T
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and b = –2.
Show that (bA)T = b AT
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and b = -2 {tex}(b A)^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {-2} & {-4} \\ {2} & {-6} \end{array}\right]^{T}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} b = -2] = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {2} \\ {-4} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}A^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} bA T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {2} \\ {-4} & {-6} \end{array}\right]=(b A)^{T}{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and b = -2
{tex}(b A)^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {-2} & {-4} \\ {2} & {-6} \end{array}\right]^{T}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} b = -2]
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {2} \\ {-4} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and {tex}A^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} bAT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {2} \\ {-4} & {-6} \end{array}\right]=(b A)^{T}{/tex}
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Show that (AB) T = B T A T
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Show that (AB)T = BT AT
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4+2} & {0+10} \\ {-4+3} & {0+15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {10} \\ {-1} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (AB) T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-1} \\ {10} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, B T A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {1} \\ {0} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4+2} & {-4+3} \\ {0+10} & {0+15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-1} \\ {10} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (AB) T Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4+2} & {0+10} \\ {-4+3} & {0+15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {10} \\ {-1} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (AB)T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-1} \\ {10} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, BT AT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {1} \\ {0} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4+2} & {-4+3} \\ {0+10} & {0+15} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {-1} \\ {10} & {15} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (AB)T
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Show that (A –B)C = AC – BC
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{C}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Show that (A –B)C = AC – BC
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} (A - B) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-4} & {2-0} \\ {-1-1} & {3-5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {2} \\ {-2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A - B)C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {2} \\ {-2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-4} & {-4} \\ {-6} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {-4} \\ {1} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {7} & {-10} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} AC - BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4-8} & {-4-0} \\ {1-7} & {-6+10} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {-4} & {-4} \\ {-6} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (A - B) C Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
(A - B) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-4} & {2-0} \\ {-1-1} & {3-5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {2} \\ {-2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (A - B)C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {2} \\ {-2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-4} & {-4} \\ {-6} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {-4} \\ {1} & {-6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {0} \\ {1} & {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {8} & {0} \\ {7} & {-10} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC - BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4-8} & {-4-0} \\ {1-7} & {-6+10} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {-4} & {-4} \\ {-6} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (A - B) C
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Show that (A – B) T = A T – B T
question_html:
Let A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], \quad \mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Show that (A – B)T = AT – BT
solution_text: We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} (A - B) T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-4} & {2-0} \\ {-1-1} & {3-5} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {2} \\ {-2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {-2} \\ {2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now A T - B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {1} \\ {0} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {-2} \\ {2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (A - B) T Hence proved
solution_html:
We have {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {2} \\ {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {0} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
(A - B)T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1-4} & {2-0} \\ {-1-1} & {3-5} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {2} \\ {-2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {-2} \\ {2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now AT - BT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-1} \\ {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll} {4} & {1} \\ {0} & {5} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-3} & {-2} \\ {2} & {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = (A - B)T
Hence proved

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then show that A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then show that A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We have A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 = A . A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta} & {\cos \theta \sin \theta+\sin \theta \cos \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta \cos \theta-\cos \theta \sin \theta} & {-\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos 2 \theta} & {2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} \\ {-2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 = A . A
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos \theta} & {\sin \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta} & {\cos \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{2} \theta} & {\cos \theta \sin \theta+\sin \theta \cos \theta} \\ {-\sin \theta \cos \theta-\cos \theta \sin \theta} & {-\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos 2 \theta} & {2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} \\ {-2 \sin \theta \cos \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and x 2 = -1, then show that (A + B) 2 = A 2 + B 2
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and x2 = -1, then show that (A + B)2 = A2 + B2
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and x 2 = -1 {tex}\therefore (A + B) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x + 1} \\ {x + 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}{(A + B)^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x + 1} \\ {x + 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x + 1} \\ {x + 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - {x^2}}&0 \\ 0&{1 - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}{A^2} = A.A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - {x^2}}&0 \\ 0&{ - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}{B^2} = B.B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}{A^2} + {B^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - {x^2} + 1}&0 \\ 0&{ - {x^2} + 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - {x^2}}&0 \\ 0&{1 - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = (A + B) 2 Hence Proved.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and x2 = -1
{tex}\therefore (A + B) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x + 1} \\ {x + 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}{(A + B)^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x + 1} \\ {x + 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x + 1} \\ {x + 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - {x^2}}&0 \\ 0&{1 - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i)
Also, {tex}{A^2} = A.A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - x} \\ x&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - {x^2}}&0 \\ 0&{ - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}{B^2} = B.B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1 \\ 1&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}{A^2} + {B^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - {x^2} + 1}&0 \\ 0&{ - {x^2} + 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - {x^2}}&0 \\ 0&{1 - {x^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = (A + B)2
Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Verify that A 2 = I, when {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Verify that A2= I, when {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {A^2} = AA} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right] = I{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right] \cdot \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 4&{ - 3}&4 \\ 3&{ - 3}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {A^2} = AA} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right] = I{/tex} Hence proved.

question_text: Prove by Mathematical Induction that (A') n = (A n )' where n {tex}\in{/tex} N for any square matrix A.
question_html: Prove by Mathematical Induction that (A')n = (An)' where n{tex}\in{/tex}N for any square matrix A.
solution_text: Let P(n) : (A') n = (A n )' Then, p(1) : (A') {tex}^1{/tex} = (A {tex}^1{/tex} )' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A' = A' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(1) is true. Now, P(k) : (A') k = (A k )', Where {tex}k \in N{/tex} Assume that p(k) is true. Then, we will prove that p(k+1) is also true. P(k + 1) : (A') k+1 = (A k+1 )' {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(k + 1) : (A') k .(A') 1 = [A k+1 ]' (A k )'.(A)' = [A k+1 ]' (AA k ) = [A k+1 ]' [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A') k = (A k )' as p(k + 1) is true and (AB)' = B'A'] (A k+1 )' = [A k+1 ]' Hence proved.
solution_html:
Let P(n) : (A')n = (An)'
Then, p(1) : (A'){tex}^1{/tex} = (A{tex}^1{/tex})'
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A' = A' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(1) is true.
Now, P(k) : (A')k = (Ak)',
Where {tex}k \in N{/tex}
Assume that p(k) is true. Then, we will prove that p(k+1) is also true.
P(k + 1) : (A')k+1 = (Ak+1)'
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P(k + 1) : (A')k.(A')1 = [Ak+1]'
(Ak)'.(A)' = [Ak+1]'
(AAk) = [Ak+1]' [{tex}\therefore{/tex} (A')k = (Ak)' as p(k + 1) is true and (AB)' = B'A']
(Ak+1)' = [Ak+1]' Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find inverse, by elementary row operations (if possible), of the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {3} \\ {-5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find inverse, by elementary row operations (if possible), of the matrix
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {3} \\ {-5} & {7} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ { - 5}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} In order to use elementary row operations we may write A = IA. {tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ { - 5}&7 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} [applying {tex}R_2\rightarrow R_2+5R_1{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&{22} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 5&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {R_2} \to \frac{1}{{22}}{R_2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ {\frac{5}{{22}}}&{\frac{1}{{22}}} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {R_1} \to {R_1} - 3{R_2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{7}{{22}}}&{\frac{{ - 3}}{{22}}} \\ {\frac{5}{{22}}}&{\frac{1}{{22}}} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{22}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 3} \\ 5&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = BA, where B is the inverse of A. {tex}\therefore B = \frac{1}{{22}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 3} \\ 5&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ { - 5}&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
In order to use elementary row operations we may write A = IA.
{tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ { - 5}&7 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} [applying {tex}R_2\rightarrow R_2+5R_1{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&{22} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 5&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because {R_2} \to \frac{1}{{22}}{R_2}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ {\frac{5}{{22}}}&{\frac{1}{{22}}} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because {R_1} \to {R_1} - 3{R_2}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{7}{{22}}}&{\frac{{ - 3}}{{22}}} \\ {\frac{5}{{22}}}&{\frac{1}{{22}}} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right] = \frac{1}{{22}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 3} \\ 5&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = BA, where B is the inverse of A.
{tex}\therefore B = \frac{1}{{22}}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&{ - 3} \\ 5&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find inverse, by elementary row operations (if possible), of the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
Find inverse, by elementary row operations (if possible), of the matrix
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {1} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {6} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} In order to use elementary row operations, we write {tex}A=I.A{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {R_2} \to {R_2} + 2{R_1}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 3} \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 2&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} Since, we obtain all zeroes in a row of the matrix A on LHS, so A -1 does not exist.
solution_html:
Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
In order to use elementary row operations, we write {tex}A=I.A{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&6 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because {R_2} \to {R_2} + 2{R_1}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 3} \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 2&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
Since, we obtain all zeroes in a row of the matrix A on LHS, so A-1 does not exist.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xy}&4 \\ {z + 6}&{x + y} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&w \\ 0&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then find values of x, y, z and w.
question_html: If  {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xy}&4 \\ {z + 6}&{x + y} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&w \\ 0&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then find values of x, y, z and w.
solution_text: We have, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xy}&4 \\ {z + 6}&{x + y} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&w \\ 0&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} By equality of matrix, x + y = 6 and xy = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6 - y and (6 - y).y = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 - 6y + 8 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 - 4y - 2y + 8 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 2)(y - 4) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 or y = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 6 - 4 = 2 or x = 6 - 4 = 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} x = 6 - y] Also, z + 6=0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -6 and w = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = 4 or x = 4, y = 2, z = -6 and w = 4
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {xy}&4 \\ {z + 6}&{x + y} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&w \\ 0&6 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
By equality of matrix, x + y = 6 and xy = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6 - y and (6 - y).y = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 - 6y + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 - 4y - 2y + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 2)(y - 4) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 or y = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 6 - 4 = 2
or x = 6 - 4 = 2 [{tex}\because{/tex} x = 6 - y]
Also, z + 6=0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = -6 and w = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2, y = 4 or x = 4, y = 2, z = -6 and w = 4

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&5 \\ 7&{12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&1 \\ 7&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , find a matrix C such that 3A + 5B + 2C is a null matrix.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&5 \\ 7&{12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&1 \\ 7&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, find a matrix C such that 3A + 5B + 2C is a null matrix.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&5 \\ 7&{12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&1 \\ 7&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Let {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3A + 5B + 2C = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3A + 5B + 2C = 3 {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&5 \\ 7&{12} \end{array}} \right] + 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&1 \\ 7&8 \end{array}} \right] + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{15} \\ {21}&{36} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {45}&5 \\ {35}&{40} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a}&{2b} \\ {2c}&{2d} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {48 + 2a}&{20 + 2b} \\ {56 + 2c}&{76 + 2d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + 48 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -24 Also, 20 + 2b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -10 56 + 2c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -28 And 76 + 2d = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = -38 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}C=\begin{bmatrix}-24&-10\\-28&-38\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&5 \\ 7&{12} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&1 \\ 7&8 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Let {tex}C = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3A + 5B + 2C = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3A + 5B + 2C = 3 {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&5 \\ 7&{12} \end{array}} \right] + 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&1 \\ 7&8 \end{array}} \right] + 2\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{15} \\ {21}&{36} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {45}&5 \\ {35}&{40} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a}&{2b} \\ {2c}&{2d} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {48 + 2a}&{20 + 2b} \\ {56 + 2c}&{76 + 2d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + 48 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -24
Also, 20 + 2b = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -10
56 + 2c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -28
And 76 + 2d = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = -38
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}C=\begin{bmatrix}-24&-10\\-28&-38\end{bmatrix}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then find A 2 - 5A - 14I. Hence, obtain A 3 .
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then find A2 - 5A - 14I. Hence, obtain A3.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i) {tex}\therefore {A^2} = A.A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {29}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 - 5A - 14I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {29}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{24} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{10} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {14}&0 \\ 0&{14} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, A 2 - 5A - 14I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A.A 2 - 5A.A - 14AI = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 3 - 5A 2 - 14A = 0 [ {tex}\because{/tex} AI = A] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 3 = 5A 2 + 14A {tex} = 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {29}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{24} \end{array}} \right] + 14\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} [Using Eqs. (i) and (ii)] {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {145}&{ - 125} \\ { - 100}&{120} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {42}&{ - 70} \\ { - 56}&{28} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {187}&{ - 195} \\ { - 156}&{148} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ....(i)
{tex}\therefore {A^2} = A.A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ { - 4}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {29}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{24} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 - 5A - 14I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {29}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{24} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{10} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {14}&0 \\ 0&{14} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, A2 - 5A - 14I = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A.A2 - 5A.A - 14AI = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A3 - 5A2 - 14A = 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} AI = A]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A3 = 5A2 + 14A
{tex} = 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {29}&{ - 25} \\ { - 20}&{24} \end{array}} \right] + 14\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{ - 5} \\ 4&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} [Using Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {145}&{ - 125} \\ { - 100}&{120} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {42}&{ - 70} \\ { - 56}&{28} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {187}&{ - 195} \\ { - 156}&{148} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the values of a, b, c and d, if {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&6 \\ { - 1}&{2d} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{a + b} \\ {c + d}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html:
Find the values of a, b, c and d, if
{tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&6 \\ { - 1}&{2d} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{a + b} \\ {c + d}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&6 \\ { - 1}&{2d} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{a + b} \\ {c + d}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3a}&{3b} \\ {3c}&{3d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4}&{6 + a + b} \\ {c + d - 1}&{3 + 2d} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a = a + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2; 3b = 6 + a + b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3b - b = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 4; 3d = 3 + 2d {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 3 and 3c = c + d - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2c = 3 - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 2, b = 4, c = 1 and d = 3
solution_html:
We have, {tex}3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b \\ c&d \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&6 \\ { - 1}&{2d} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{a + b} \\ {c + d}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {3a}&{3b} \\ {3c}&{3d} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {a + 4}&{6 + a + b} \\ {c + d - 1}&{3 + 2d} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a = a + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2;
3b = 6 + a + b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3b - b = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 4;
3d = 3 + 2d {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 3
and 3c = c + d - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2c = 3 - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 2, b = 4, c = 1 and d = 3

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the matrix A such that {tex}\left[ \begin{gathered} \;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;1}&{\;\;0} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&{\;\;4} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .
question_html: Find the matrix A such that {tex}\left[ \begin{gathered} \;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;1}&{\;\;0} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&{\;\;4} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}\left[ \begin{gathered} \;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;1}&{\;\;0} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&{\;\;4} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} From the given equation, it is clear that order of A should be {tex}2\times 3{/tex} Let {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b&c \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} d&e&f \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}2&-1\\1&0\\-3&4\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}a&b&c\\d&e&f\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}-1&-8&-10\\1&-2&-5\\9&22&15\end{bmatrix}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a - d}&{2b - e}&{2c - f} \\ {a + 0d}&{b + 0.e}&{c + 0.f} \\ { - 3a + 4d}&{ - 3b + 4e}&{ - 3c + 4f} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a - d}&{2b - e}&{2c - f} \\ a&b&c \\ { - 3a + 4d}&{ - 3b + 4e}&{ - 3c + 4f} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} By equality of matrices, we get a = 1, b = -2, c = -5 and 2a - d = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 2a + 1 = 3; {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2b - e = -8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = 2(-2) + 8 =4 2c - f = -10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f = 2c + 10 = 0 {tex}\therefore A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{\;\;4}&{\;\;0} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\left[ \begin{gathered} \;\;\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\;\;1}&{\;\;0} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&{\;\;4} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
From the given equation, it is clear that order of A should be {tex}2\times 3{/tex}
Let {tex}A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} a&b&c \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} d&e&f \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\begin{bmatrix}2&-1\\1&0\\-3&4\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}a&b&c\\d&e&f\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}-1&-8&-10\\1&-2&-5\\9&22&15\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a - d}&{2b - e}&{2c - f} \\ {a + 0d}&{b + 0.e}&{c + 0.f} \\ { - 3a + 4d}&{ - 3b + 4e}&{ - 3c + 4f} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2a - d}&{2b - e}&{2c - f} \\ a&b&c \\ { - 3a + 4d}&{ - 3b + 4e}&{ - 3c + 4f} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&{ - 8}&{ - 10} \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \\ 9&{22}&{15} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
By equality of matrices, we get
a = 1, b = -2, c = -5
and 2a - d = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 2a + 1 = 3;
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2b - e = -8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = 2(-2) + 8 =4
2c - f = -10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f = 2c + 10 = 0
{tex}\therefore A = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 2}&{ - 5} \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&{\;\;4}&{\;\;0} \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then find A 2 + 2A + 7I.
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then find A2 + 2A + 7I.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow {A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 8}&{2 + 2} \\ {4 + 4}&{8 + 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&4 \\ 8&9 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {A^2} + 2A + 7 = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&4 \\ 8&9 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4 \\ 8&2 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {18}&8 \\ {16}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow {A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ 4&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 + 8}&{2 + 2} \\ {4 + 4}&{8 + 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&4 \\ 8&9 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {A^2} + 2A + 7 = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&4 \\ 8&9 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4 \\ 8&2 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {18}&8 \\ {16}&{18} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
