book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{\sin \alpha } \\ { - \sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and A -1 = A', then find value of {tex}\alpha.{/tex}
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{\sin \alpha } \\ { - \sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and A-1 = A', then find value of {tex}\alpha.{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{\sin \alpha } \\ { - \sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Also, A -1 = A' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AA -1 = AA' {tex}\Rightarrow I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{\sin \alpha } \\ { - \sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{{\cos }^2}\alpha + {{\sin }^2}\alpha }&0 \\ 0&{{{\sin }^2}\alpha + {{\cos }^2}\alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} By using equality of matrices, we get {tex}{\cos ^2}\alpha + {\sin ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex} which is true for all real values of {tex}\alpha{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \alpha{/tex} has every real value.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{\sin \alpha } \\ { - \sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Also, A-1 = A'
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AA-1 = AA'
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{\sin \alpha } \\ { - \sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos \alpha }&{ - \sin \alpha } \\ {\sin \alpha }&{\cos \alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{{\cos }^2}\alpha + {{\sin }^2}\alpha }&0 \\ 0&{{{\sin }^2}\alpha + {{\cos }^2}\alpha } \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
By using equality of matrices, we get
{tex}{\cos ^2}\alpha + {\sin ^2}\alpha = 1{/tex}
which is true for all real values of {tex}\alpha{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \alpha{/tex} has every real value.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If the matrix {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&3 \\ 2&b&{ - 1} \\ c&1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} is a skew symmetric matrix, then find the values of a, b and c.
question_html: If the matrix {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&3 \\ 2&b&{ - 1} \\ c&1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} is a skew symmetric matrix, then find the values of a, b and c.
solution_text: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&3 \\ 2&b&{ - 1} \\ c&1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Since, A is skew-symmetric matrix. {tex}\therefore{/tex} A' = -A {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2&c \\ a&b&1 \\ 3&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&3 \\ 2&b&{ - 1} \\ c&1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2&c \\ a&b&1 \\ 3&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&{ - b}&{ + 1} \\ { - c}&{ - 1}&{ - 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} By equality of matrices, we get a = -2, c = -3 and b = -b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = -2, b = 0 and c = -3
solution_html:
Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&3 \\ 2&b&{ - 1} \\ c&1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Since, A is skew-symmetric matrix.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A' = -A
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2&c \\ a&b&1 \\ 3&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&a&3 \\ 2&b&{ - 1} \\ c&1&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2&c \\ a&b&1 \\ 3&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - a}&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&{ - b}&{ + 1} \\ { - c}&{ - 1}&{ - 0} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
By equality of matrices, we get
a = -2, c = -3 and b = -b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = -2, b = 0 and c = -3

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}P(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then show that P(x).P(y) = P(x + y) = P(y).P(x).
question_html: If {tex}P(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then show that P(x).P(y) = P(x + y) = P(y).P(x).
solution_text: we have, {tex}P(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right],{/tex} {tex}\therefore P(y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{\sin y} \\ { - \sin y}&{\cos y} \end{array}} \right],{/tex} Now, {tex}P(x).P(y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{\sin y} \\ { - \sin y}&{\cos y} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x.\cos y - \sin x\sin y}&{\cos x.\sin y + \sin x.\cos y} \\ { - \sin x.\cos y - \cos x.\sin y}&{ - \sin x.\sin y + \cos x.\cos y} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{\sin (x + y)} \\ { - \sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ….(i) {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\therefore \cos (x + y) = \cos x.\cos y - \sin x.\sin y} \\ {and\;\sin (x + y) = \sin x.\cos y + \cos x.\sin y} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}P(x + y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{\sin (x + y)} \\ { - \sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .....(ii) Also, P(y).P(x) {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{\sin y} \\ { - \sin y}&{\cos y} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y.\cos x - \sin y.\sin x}&{\cos y.\sin x + \sin y.\cos x} \\ { - \sin y.\cos x - \sin x - \cos y}&{ - \sin y.\sin x + \cos y.\cos x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{\sin (x + y)} \\ { - \sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Thus, we see from the Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii) that, P(x).P(y) = P(x + y) = P(y).P(x) Hence Proved.
solution_html:
we have,
{tex}P(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right],{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P(y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{\sin y} \\ { - \sin y}&{\cos y} \end{array}} \right],{/tex}
Now, {tex}P(x).P(y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{\sin y} \\ { - \sin y}&{\cos y} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x.\cos y - \sin x\sin y}&{\cos x.\sin y + \sin x.\cos y} \\ { - \sin x.\cos y - \cos x.\sin y}&{ - \sin x.\sin y + \cos x.\cos y} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{\sin (x + y)} \\ { - \sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ….(i)
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\therefore \cos (x + y) = \cos x.\cos y - \sin x.\sin y} \\ {and\;\sin (x + y) = \sin x.\cos y + \cos x.\sin y} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}P(x + y) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{\sin (x + y)} \\ { - \sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} .....(ii)
Also, P(y).P(x) {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y}&{\sin y} \\ { - \sin y}&{\cos y} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos x}&{\sin x} \\ { - \sin x}&{\cos x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos y.\cos x - \sin y.\sin x}&{\cos y.\sin x + \sin y.\cos x} \\ { - \sin y.\cos x - \sin x - \cos y}&{ - \sin y.\sin x + \cos y.\cos x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\cos (x + y)}&{\sin (x + y)} \\ { - \sin (x + y)}&{\cos (x + y)} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Thus, we see from the Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii) that,
P(x).P(y) = P(x + y) = P(y).P(x) Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A is square matrix such that A 2 = A, show that (I + A) 3 = 7A + I.
question_html: If A is square matrix such that A2 = A, show that (I + A)3= 7A + I.
solution_text: Since, A 2 = A and (I + A).(I + A) = I 2 + IA + AI + A 2 = I 2 + 2.AI + A 2 = I + 2A + A = I + 3A And (I + A).(I + A)(I + A) = (I + A)(I + 3A) = I 2 + 3AI + AI + 3A 2 = I + 4AI + 3A = I + 7A = 7A + I Hence proved.
solution_html:
Since, A2 = A and (I + A).(I + A) = I2 + IA + AI + A2
= I2 + 2.AI + A2
= I + 2A + A = I + 3A
And (I + A).(I + A)(I + A) = (I + A)(I + 3A)
= I2 + 3AI + AI + 3A2
= I + 4AI + 3A
= I + 7A = 7A + I
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A, B are square matrices of same order and B is a skew - symmetric matrix, show that A′BA is skew symmetric.
question_html: If A, B are square matrices of same order and B is a skew - symmetric matrix, show that A′BA is skew symmetric.
solution_text: Since, A and B are square matrices of same order and B is a skew-symmetric matrix {tex}\therefore {/tex} B' = -B .....(1) Now, we have to prove that A’BA is a skew-symmetric matrix. (A'BA)' = [A'(BA)]'=(BA)'(A')' ...[ {tex}\therefore{/tex} (AB)'=B'A'] = (A'B')A. [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} (A')'=A] = A'(-B)A [using (1)] = -(A'BA) Hence, A'BA is a skew-symmetric matrix.
solution_html:
Since, A and B are square matrices of same order and B is a skew-symmetric matrix
{tex}\therefore {/tex} B' = -B   .....(1)
Now, we have to prove that A’BA is a skew-symmetric matrix.
(A'BA)' = [A'(BA)]'=(BA)'(A')'  ...[{tex}\therefore{/tex}(AB)'=B'A']
= (A'B')A. [{tex}\therefore{/tex} (A')'=A]
= A'(-B)A [using (1)]
= -(A'BA)
Hence, A'BA is a skew-symmetric matrix.

question_text: If AB = BA for any two square matrices, prove by mathematical induction that (AB) n = A n B n .
question_html: If AB = BA for any two square matrices, prove by mathematical induction that (AB)n = An Bn.
solution_text: For n = 1,we have, AB 1 = B 1 A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = BA, which is true. Let it be true for n = m i.e AB m = BA m {tex}{/tex} .........(1) Then,for n = m + 1, AB m+1 = A(B m B) = (AB m )B = (B m A)B [by (1)] = B m (AB) = (B m B)A = B m+1 A. So ,it is true for n=m+1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} AB n = B n A
solution_html:
For n = 1,we have, AB1 = B1A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = BA, which is true.
Let it be true for n = m i.e ABm = BAm{tex}{/tex}.........(1)
Then,for n = m + 1,
ABm+1 = A(Bm B) = (ABm)B = (BmA)B [by (1)]
= Bm(AB) = (BmB)A = Bm+1A.
So ,it is true for n=m+1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ABn = BnA

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the values of x, y, z if the matrix {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} satisfy the equation A'A = I.
question_html: Find the values of x, y, z if the matrix {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} satisfy the equation A'A = I.
solution_text: It is given that: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} From the given question, A'.A = I {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + {x^2} + {x^2}}&{0 + xy - xy}&{0 - xy + xz} \\ {0 + xy - xy}&{4{y^2} + {y^2} + {y^2}}&{2yz - yz - yz} \\ {0 - zx + zx}&{2yz - yz - yz}&{{z^2} + {z^2} + {z^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2}}&0&0 \\ 0&{6{y^2}}&0 \\ 0&0&{3{z^2}} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating corresponding entries,we obtain 2x 2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} And 6y 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow {y^2} = \frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex} And 3z 2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\;Z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{2y}&z \\ x&y&{ - z} \\ x&{ - y}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
From the given question, A'.A = I {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&x&x \\ {2y}&y&{ - y} \\ z&{ - z}&z \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {0 + {x^2} + {x^2}}&{0 + xy - xy}&{0 - xy + xz} \\ {0 + xy - xy}&{4{y^2} + {y^2} + {y^2}}&{2yz - yz - yz} \\ {0 - zx + zx}&{2yz - yz - yz}&{{z^2} + {z^2} + {z^2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2{x^2}}&0&0 \\ 0&{6{y^2}}&0 \\ 0&0&{3{z^2}} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Equating corresponding entries,we obtain
2x2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
And 6y2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow {y^2} = \frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex}
And 3z2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {z^2} = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;y = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }},\;Z = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If possible, using elementary row transformation, find the inverse of the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-5} & {3} & {1} \\ {-3} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
If possible, using elementary row transformation, find the inverse of the matrix
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-5} & {3} & {1} \\ {-3} & {2} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: For getting the inverse of the given matrix A by row elementary operations we may write the given matrix as A=IA {tex}\because \;\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&3 \\ { - 5}&3&1 \\ { - 3}&2&3 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{R_2} \to {R_2} + {R_1}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&3 \\ { - 3}&2&4 \\ { - 3}&2&3 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 1&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \;{R_3} \to {R_3} - {R_2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&3 \\ { - 3}&2&4 \\ 0&0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 1&1&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{R_1} \to {R_1} + {R_2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&1&7 \\ { - 3}&2&4 \\ 0&0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&0 \\ 1&1&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \;{R_2} \to {R_2} - 3{R_1}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&1&7 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 17} \\ 0&0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&0 \\ { - 5}&{ - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because {R_1} \to {R_1} + {R_2}} \\ {and\;{R_3} \to - 1.{R_3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 10} \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 17} \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&{ - 1}&0 \\ { - 5}&{ - 2}&0 \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because \;{R_1} \to {R_1} + 10{R_3}} \\ {and\;{R_2} \to {R_2} + 17{R_3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&0 \\ 0&{ - 1}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&9&{ - 10} \\ {12}&{15}&{ - 17} \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because \;{R_1} \to - 1{R_1}} \\ {and\;{R_2} \to - 1{R_2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&{ - 9}&{10} \\ { - 12}&{ - 15}&{17} \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} So, the inverse of A is {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&{ - 9}&{10} \\ { - 12}&{ - 15}&{17} \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
For getting the inverse of the given matrix A by row elementary operations we may write the given matrix as A=IA
{tex}\because \;\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&3 \\ { - 5}&3&1 \\ { - 3}&2&3 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {{R_2} \to {R_2} + {R_1}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&3 \\ { - 3}&2&4 \\ { - 3}&2&3 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 1&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because \;{R_3} \to {R_3} - {R_2}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{ - 1}&3 \\ { - 3}&2&4 \\ 0&0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 1&1&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {{R_1} \to {R_1} + {R_2}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&1&7 \\ { - 3}&2&4 \\ 0&0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&0 \\ 1&1&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because \;{R_2} \to {R_2} - 3{R_1}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&1&7 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 17} \\ 0&0&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&0 \\ { - 5}&{ - 2}&0 \\ { - 1}&{ - 1}&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because {R_1} \to {R_1} + {R_2}} \\ {and\;{R_3} \to - 1.{R_3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&{ - 10} \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 17} \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 3}&{ - 1}&0 \\ { - 5}&{ - 2}&0 \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because \;{R_1} \to {R_1} + 10{R_3}} \\ {and\;{R_2} \to {R_2} + 17{R_3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&0&0 \\ 0&{ - 1}&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&9&{ - 10} \\ {12}&{15}&{ - 17} \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because \;{R_1} \to - 1{R_1}} \\ {and\;{R_2} \to - 1{R_2}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&{ - 9}&{10} \\ { - 12}&{ - 15}&{17} \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
So, the inverse of A is {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&{ - 9}&{10} \\ { - 12}&{ - 15}&{17} \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If possible, using elementary row transformation, find the inverse of the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {3} & {-3} \\ {-1} & {-2} & {2} \\ {1} & {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
If possible, using elementary row transformation, find the inverse of the matrix
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {3} & {-3} \\ {-1} & {-2} & {2} \\ {1} & {1} & {-1} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: For getting the inverse of the given matrix A by row elementary operations we may write the given matrix as A=IA {tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{ - 3} \\ { - 1}&{ - 2}&2 \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because \;{R_2} \to {R_2} + {R_3}} \\ {and\;{R_1} \to {R_1} - 2{R_3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 0&{ - 1}&1 \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 2} \\ 0&1&1 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \;{R_2} \to {R_2} + {R_1}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 0&0&0 \\ 1&1&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 2} \\ 2&1&{ - 2} \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex} Since the second row of matrix A on LHS is containing all zeroes, so we can say that the inverse of matrix A does not exist.
solution_html:
For getting the inverse of the given matrix A by row elementary operations we may write the given matrix as A=IA
{tex}\therefore \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&{ - 3} \\ { - 1}&{ - 2}&2 \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\because \;{R_2} \to {R_2} + {R_3}} \\ {and\;{R_1} \to {R_1} - 2{R_3}} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 0&{ - 1}&1 \\ 1&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 2} \\ 0&1&1 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because \;{R_2} \to {R_2} + {R_1}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - 1} \\ 0&0&0 \\ 1&1&1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&{ - 2} \\ 2&1&{ - 2} \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]A{/tex}
Since the second row of matrix A on LHS is containing all zeroes, so we can say that the inverse of matrix A does not exist.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If possible, using elementary row transformation, find the inverse of the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {5} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_html:
If possible, using elementary row transformation, find the inverse of the matrix
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {5} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: We may write the given matrix as A = IA {tex}\therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {5} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex} [Applying R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 2 - (5/2) R 1 ] {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {1} & {5 / 2} \\ {0} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {-5 / 2} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex} [Applying R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 3 - R 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {1} & {5 / 2} \\ {0} & {0} & {1 / 2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {-5 / 2} & {1} & {0} \\ {5 / 2} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex} [Applying R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 2 - 5R 3 ] {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1 / 2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {5 / 2} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex} [Applying R 1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 1 + 2R 3 ] {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1 / 2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {5 / 2} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex} [Applying R 1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} R 1 and R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2R 3 ] {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {1} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {-5} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right] A{/tex} Hence, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {1} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {-5} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} is the inverse of given matrix A.
solution_html:
We may write the given matrix as
A = IA
{tex}\therefore \quad\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {5} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex}
[Applying R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 - (5/2) R1]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {1} & {5 / 2} \\ {0} & {1} & {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {-5 / 2} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex}
[Applying R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R3 - R2]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {1} & {5 / 2} \\ {0} & {0} & {1 / 2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {-5 / 2} & {1} & {0} \\ {5 / 2} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex}
[Applying R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 - 5R3]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {0} & {-1} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1 / 2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {5 / 2} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex}
[Applying R1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R1 + 2R3]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} {2} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1 / 2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {6} & {-2} & {2} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {5 / 2} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right] A{/tex}
[Applying R1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}R1 and R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2R3]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} {1} & {0} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {1} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {-5} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right] A{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {3} & {-1} & {1} \\ {-15} & {6} & {-5} \\ {-5} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} is the inverse of given matrix A.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Express the matrix {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&1 \\ 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix.
question_html: Express the matrix {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&1 \\ 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix.
solution_text: We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&1 \\ 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&4 \\ 3&{ - 1}&1 \\ 1&2&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{A + A'}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&4&5 \\ 4&{ - 2}&3 \\ 5&3&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&2&{\frac{5}{2}} \\ 2&{ - 1}&{\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{5}{2}}&{\frac{3}{2}}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} And {tex}\frac{{A + A'}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&0&1 \\ 3&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}} \\ { - 1}&0&{\frac{1}{2}} \\ {\frac{3}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow A = \frac{{A + A'}}{2} + \frac{{A - A'}}{2}{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&2&{\frac{5}{2}} \\ 2&{ - 1}&{\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{5}{2}}&{\frac{3}{2}}&2 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - \frac{3}{2}} \\ { - 1}&0&{\frac{1}{2}} \\ {\frac{3}{2}}&{ - \frac{1}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} which is the required expression.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3&1 \\ 1&{ - 1}&2 \\ 4&1&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&1&4 \\ 3&{ - 1}&1 \\ 1&2&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now,  {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{A + A'}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&4&5 \\ 4&{ - 2}&3 \\ 5&3&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&2&{\frac{5}{2}} \\ 2&{ - 1}&{\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{5}{2}}&{\frac{3}{2}}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}\frac{{A + A'}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&2&{ - 3} \\ { - 2}&0&1 \\ 3&{ - 1}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}= \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}} \\ { - 1}&0&{\frac{1}{2}} \\ {\frac{3}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 1}}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow A = \frac{{A + A'}}{2} + \frac{{A - A'}}{2}{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&2&{\frac{5}{2}} \\ 2&{ - 1}&{\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{5}{2}}&{\frac{3}{2}}&2 \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&1&{ - \frac{3}{2}} \\ { - 1}&0&{\frac{1}{2}} \\ {\frac{3}{2}}&{ - \frac{1}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
which is the required expression.

question_text: If A = {tex}\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin ^{-1}(x \pi)} & {\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} \\ {\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} & {\cot ^{-1}(\pi x)} \end{array}\right],{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-\cos ^{-1}(x \pi)} & {\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} \\ {\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} & {-\tan ^{-1}(\pi x)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then A - B is equal to
question_html: If A = {tex}\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\sin ^{-1}(x \pi)} & {\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} \\ {\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} & {\cot ^{-1}(\pi x)} \end{array}\right],{/tex} {tex} \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-\cos ^{-1}(x \pi)} & {\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} \\ {\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right)} & {-\tan ^{-1}(\pi x)} \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then A - B is equal to

options.explanation: In the given question, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-\frac{1}{\pi} \cos ^{-1} x \pi} & {\frac{1}{\pi} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}} \\ {\frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}} & {-\frac{1}{\pi} \tan ^{-1} \pi x} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {\frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1} x \pi} & {\frac{1}{\pi} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}} \\ {\frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}} & {\frac{1}{\pi} \cot ^{-1} \pi x} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A - B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\sin ^{-1} x \pi+\cos ^{-1} x \pi\right)} & {0} \\ {0} & {\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\cot ^{-1} \pi x+\tan ^{-1} \pi x\right)} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {\frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}} & {0} \\ {0} & {\frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{2} I{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Construct a matrix {tex}A = {[{a_{ij}}]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} whose elements a ij are given by a ij = e 2ix sin jx
question_html: Construct a matrix {tex}A = {[{a_{ij}}]_{2 \times 2}}{/tex} whose elements aij are given by aij = e2ixsin jx
solution_text: For i = 1, j = 1, a 11 = e 2x sinx For i = 1, j = 2, a 12 = e 2x sin 2x For i = 2, j = 1, a 21 = e 4x sin x For i = 2, j = 2, a 22 = e 4x sin 2x Thus, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{e^{2x}}\sin x}&{{e^{2x}}\sin 2x} \\ {{e^{4x}}\sin x}&{{e^{4x}}\sin 2x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
For i = 1, j = 1, a11 = e2x sinx
For i = 1, j = 2, a12 = e2x sin 2x
For i = 2, j = 1, a21 = e4x sin x
For i = 2, j = 2, a22 = e4x sin 2x
Thus, {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{e^{2x}}\sin x}&{{e^{2x}}\sin 2x} \\ {{e^{4x}}\sin x}&{{e^{4x}}\sin 2x} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ 1&2 \end{array}} \right],\;B = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&3&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right],\;C = \left[ {\frac{1}{2}} \right],{/tex}{tex}D = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&6&8 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 5&7&9 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}, then which of the sums A + B, B + C, C + D and B + D is defined?
solution_text: Only B + D is defined since matrices of the same order can only be added.
solution_html: Only B + D is defined since matrices of the same order can only be added.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Show that a matrix which is both symmetric and skew symmetric is a zero matrix.
question_html: Show that a matrix which is both symmetric and skew symmetric is a zero matrix.
solution_text: Let A = [a ij ] be a matrix that is both symmetric and skew-symmetric. Since A is a skew-symmetric matrix, so A′ = -A. Thus, for all i and j, we have a ij = -a ji .................... (1) Again, since A is a symmetric matrix, so A′ = A. Thus, for all i and j, we have a ji = a ij ......... (2) Therefore, from (1) and (2), we get a ij = -a ij for all i and j Or 2a ij = 0, i.e., a ij = 0 for all i and j. Hence A is Zero matrix.
solution_html:
Let A = [aij] be a matrix that is both symmetric and skew-symmetric.
Since A is a skew-symmetric matrix, so A′ = -A.
Thus, for all i and j, we have aij = -aji ....................(1)
Again, since A is a symmetric matrix, so A′ = A.
Thus, for all i and j, we have
aji = aij ......... (2)
Therefore, from (1) and (2), we get
aij = -aij for all i and j
Or 2aij = 0,
i.e., aij = 0 for all i and j.
Hence A is Zero matrix.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 8 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} , find the value of x.
question_html: If {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 8 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}, find the value of x.
solution_text: We have {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 8 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x - 9}&{4x} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 8 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [2x 2 - 9x + 32x] = [0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [2x 2 + 23x] = [0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 +23x = 0 Or x(2x + 23) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,\;x = \frac{{ - 23}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x}&3 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&2 \\ { - 3}&0 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 8 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x - 9}&{4x} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ 8 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [2x2 - 9x + 32x] = [0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [2x2 + 23x] = [0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 +23x = 0
Or x(2x + 23) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,\;x = \frac{{ - 23}}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A is 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 3 invertible matrix, then show that for any scalar k(non-zero), kA is invertible and {tex}{(kA)^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}{/tex}
question_html: If A is 3{tex} \times {/tex}3 invertible matrix, then show that for any scalar k(non-zero), kA is invertible and {tex}{(kA)^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}(kA)\left( {\frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}} \right) = \left( {k.\frac{1}{k}} \right)\left( {A.{A^{ - 1}}} \right) = 1(I) = I{/tex} Hence (kA) is inverse of {tex}\left( {\frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} or {tex}{(kA)^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}(kA)\left( {\frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}} \right) = \left( {k.\frac{1}{k}} \right)\left( {A.{A^{ - 1}}} \right) = 1(I) = I{/tex}
Hence (kA) is inverse of {tex}\left( {\frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}} \right){/tex} or {tex}{(kA)^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{k}{A^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Express the matrix A as the sum of a symmetric and a skew symmetric matrix, where {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&{ - 6} \\ 7&3&5 \\ 1&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
question_html:
Express the matrix A as the sum of a symmetric and a skew symmetric matrix, where
{tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&{ - 6} \\ 7&3&5 \\ 1&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&{ - 6} \\ 7&3&5 \\ 1&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then {tex}A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&7&1 \\ 4&3&{ - 2} \\ { - 6}&5&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{{A + A'}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{11}&{ - 5} \\ {11}&6&3 \\ { - 5}&3&8 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{\frac{{11}}{2}}&{\frac{-5}{2}} \\ {\frac{{11}}{2}}&3&{\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 5}}{2}}&{\frac{3}{2}}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
And {tex}\frac{{A - A'}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - 3}&{ - 7} \\ 3&0&7 \\ 7&{ - 7}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \frac{3}{2}}&{ - \frac{7}{2}} \\ {\frac{3}{2}}&0&{\frac{7}{2}} \\ {\frac{7}{2}}&{ - \frac{7}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}\frac{{A + A'}}{2} + \frac{{A - A'}}{2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{\frac{{11}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 5}}{2}} \\ {\frac{{11}}{2}}&3&{\frac{3}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 5}}{2}}&{\frac{3}{2}}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}+ \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 7}}{2}} \\ {\frac{3}{2}}&0&{\frac{7}{2}} \\ {\frac{7}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 7}}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&4&{ - 6} \\ 7&3&5 \\ 1&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right] = A{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 1&2&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then show that A satisfies the equation A 3 - 4A 2 - 3A+ 11I = 0
question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 1&2&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then show that A satisfies the equation A3 - 4A2 - 3A+ 11I = 0
solution_html:
{tex}A^2=AA=\begin{bmatrix}1&3&2\\2&0&-1\\1&2&3\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&3&2\\2&0&-1\\1&2&3\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}9&7&5\\1&4&1\\8&9&9\end{bmatrix}{/tex}
{tex}{A^3} = {A^2} \times A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&7&5 \\ 1&4&1 \\ 8&9&9 \end{array}} \right] \times \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 1&2&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 + 14 + 5}&{27 + 0 + 10}&{18 - 7 + 15} \\ {1 + 8 + 1}&{3 + 0 + 2}&{2 - 4 + 3} \\ {8 + 18 + 9}&{24 + 0 + 18}&{16 - 9 + 27} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {28}&{37}&{26} \\ {10}&5&1 \\ {35}&{42}&{34} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now A3 - 4A2 - 3A + 11(I)
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {28}&{37}&{26} \\ {10}&5&1 \\ {35}&{42}&{34} \end{array}} \right] - 4\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 9&7&5 \\ 1&4&1 \\ 8&9&9 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex} - 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 1&2&3 \end{array}} \right] + 11\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&0 \\ 0&1&0 \\ 0&0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {28 - 36 - 3 + 11}&{37 - 28 - 9 + 0}&{26 - 20 - 6 + 0} \\ {10 - 4 - 6 + 0}&{5 - 16 + 0 + 11}&{1 - 4 + 3 + 0} \\ {35 - 32 - 3 + 0}&{42 - 36 - 6 + 0}&{34 - 36 - 9 + 11} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \\ 0&0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} . Then show that A 2 - 4A + 7I = 0. Using this result calculate A 5 also.
question_html: Let {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}. Then show that A2 - 4A + 7I = 0. Using this result calculate A5 also.
solution_text: We have {tex}{A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{12} \\ { - 4}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} - 4A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8}&{ - 12} \\ 4&{ - 8} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}7I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{A^2} - 4A + 7I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - 8 + 7}&{12 - 12 + 0} \\ { - 4 + 4 + 0}&{1 - 8 + 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = 4A - 7I Thus A 3 = AA 2 = A(4A - 7I) = 4(4A - 7I) - 7A = 16A - 28I - 7A = 9A - 28I And so A 5 = A 3 A 2 = (9A - 28I) (4A - 7I) = 36A 2 - 63A - 112A + 196I = 36 (4A - 7I) - 175A + 196I = - 31A - 56I {tex} = - 31\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] - 56\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 118}&{ - 93} \\ {31}&{ - 118} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}{A^2} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{12} \\ { - 4}&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} - 4A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 8}&{ - 12} \\ 4&{ - 8} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} and {tex}7I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}{A^2} - 4A + 7I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1 - 8 + 7}&{12 - 12 + 0} \\ { - 4 + 4 + 0}&{1 - 8 + 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A2 = 4A - 7I
Thus A3 = AA2 = A(4A - 7I) = 4(4A - 7I) - 7A
= 16A - 28I - 7A = 9A - 28I
And so A5 = A3A2
= (9A - 28I) (4A - 7I)
= 36A2- 63A - 112A + 196I
= 36 (4A - 7I) - 175A + 196I
= - 31A - 56I
{tex} = - 31\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&3 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] - 56\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 118}&{ - 93} \\ {31}&{ - 118} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-4} & {5} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {-2} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {2} & {-1} & {3} \\ {-4} & {5} & {1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {3} \\ {4} & {-2} \\ {1} & {5} \end{array}\right]{/tex} then

options.text: AB and BA both are defined
options.explanation: In given matrix order of A = {tex}2 \times 3{/tex} order of B {tex}=3 \times 2{/tex} AB will be defined if the number of column in A is equal to the number of rows in B so, {tex}\left(A_{2 \times 3}\right)(B_{3 \times 2})=A B_{2 \times 2}{/tex} Similarly {tex}\left(B_{3 \times 2}\right)\left(A_{2 \times 3}\right)=BA_{3 \times 3}{/tex} Thus, Both AB and BA are defined.

options.text: AB and BA both are not defined
options.html: AB and BA both are not defined

correct_option_text: AB and BA both are defined

options.explanation: square matrix, In a given matrix the number of rows is equal to the number of columns.

question_text: If A and B are symmetric matrices of the same order, then (AB′ –BA′) is a
question_html: If A and B are symmetric matrices of the same order, then (AB′ –BA′) is a

options.explanation: Given:(AB′ –BA′)′ = (AB′)′ – (BA′)′ = (AB)' - (BA)' ...( {tex}\because {/tex} A' = A and B' = B) = B'A' - A'B' = BA' - AB' = - (AB' - BA' ) Therefore, we obtain(AB′ –BA′) is Skew-Symmetric.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Matrix subtraction is associative.
question_html: Matrix subtraction is associative.

options.explanation: It is false, because for matrices A, B, C of same order, (A − B) − C {tex}\ne{/tex} A − (B − C) {tex}\because{/tex} A − (B − C) = A − B + C and (A − B) − C = A − B − C So, Matrix subtraction is not associative.
